Dan Kennedy’s
www.dankennedy.com

Marketing To The Affluent
Dan: There’s a couple of boxes back there on the table. One of them has
a manual in it that looks like this. It says Marketing to the Affluent on the
front. You might want one of those. The other box does not have enough
for everybody, so don’t get them. The other box just has copies of my
political web site for those that are curious and haven’t seen it.
I can see we’re going to have trouble this weekend. We have four people
passing out to this side and no people passing them out to this side. This
is going to be a long two days.
I apologize in advance. I don’t believe I am contagious. My fever
stopped about 2:00 in the afternoon yesterday. I’m fairly ill. I’ve got a
pretty bad cold and a sore throat and a headache.
[Off mic]: Are you pregnant?
Dan: Well see now that would solve every economic problem. There is
that guy this year that somehow got pregnant. Didn’t he? You think
there’d be a reality show in that? Wouldn’t you? Was he on Oprah? See
that doesn’t do you any good without a book. You see an Oprah
appearance without a book in the store. What good is that? You would
think there would be a reality show in that deal. Nine episodes, one a
month, follow him around.
No, I’m not pregnant. I’m sick. Obviously, I had no choice and therefore
you had no choice. I saw no point in notifying you yesterday. We’ll do
the best we can.
You should have right inside your little manual an agenda which I will
quickly flesh out for you, so you know what’s going on. In a moment
we’ll get acquainted. From 9:00 to 9:45 we will have a session, the first
one being on price suspension. Take a quick little break and at 10:00 a.m.
we’re getting into limos.
The first place we’re going is downtown to Playhouse Square. The gal in
charge of donor development, meaning getting money out of people, will
give us a tour. Then you can pick her brain about how you find people to
pony up $25,000, $50,000, $100,000 every year in order to be in the donor
circle and who those people are. How you keep them year to year. How
you manage to find them in Cleveland, which if you watch the news there
should be none of them.
Theoretically everybody here’s living in a cardboard box if you watch the

news. That’s actually not the case. We’ll take half an hour to get there.
We’ll be there roughly 10:30 a.m. or so, until 11:30 a.m., 11:45 a.m. Then
we will hustle back here as quickly as we can. Lunch will be waiting for
us when we come back. We have a couple of sessions then in the
afternoon with a break in the middle from 3:45 p.m. to 4:00 p.m.
Hern, you’re going to quickly do a little presentation on your thing. Wrap
up at 4:30, give you a little break. We’re boarding limos at 5:00 We’re
going to Blue Canyon Restaurant. A few of you may have eaten there at
different times in meetings.
One of the two owners and their sales and marketing director and I think
their training person are all going to be with us for a brief period of time.
I’ll talk to you a little bit more about that before we go over there. That’s
our day today.
Tomorrow we start right at 8:00 in the morning. We have sessions and a
hot seat session with two of you. Where are the photography studio
people in the room? Oh good, and you managed to sit together. See,
usually the attorneys do that. We’re using you guys tomorrow and
working on your business.
At 10:00 we will board limos and go over to the Pet Hotel and Spa and
take a tour. You’ll have an opportunity to talk to the guy that owns that
thing. We end tomorrow at 3:30. You have then a two hour break,
change clothes, relax, talk to each other, whatever you want to do. You’ll
board limos at 5:30, those of you that are going to the track. I need to find
out about that in a minute. Well, let me do that now. How many of you
are going? Okay, now let me see it the other way. Who’s not going?
One, two, three, four, five, six, seven okay.
For those of you that are going you can think about this. We can figure it
out tomorrow. I have your transportation back right now scheduled for
10:00. We have of ours only two horses in. One is in the twelfth race and
one is in the thirteenth race, both of which are later than 10:00. If you
chose to stay to watch those, you would be there until about 11:20 to
11:30 before you got out of there. I don’t think either one of them has a
prayer in hell tomorrow night if that affects your decision. We’ll probably
run that as something of a democracy. You can think about it, but you do
all have to leave together. We’ll have to make a decision about that.
That’s kind of where we’re going to go. I have some option, flexibility in
what we do in sessions kind of depending on what I hear from you guys
this morning and as we go.
I do need to mention that obviously we are recording. I don’t know what

we’re going to do with it, but we’re going to do something. Therefore, if
you don’t want to be recorded now is a good time to leave. We’ll be
happy to issue you a refund but otherwise you are on tape and possibly
published on tape throughout the two days.
Let’s do the who, what, where stuff. What I need everybody to hear from
you is of course who you are, the thumbnail description of your business.
Meaning something shorter then birth to present, but enough that people
get a handle on what it is that you do. The main three reasons you’ re here.
The three things you’re hunting for. The three things you hope to
accomplish. The three big questions, puzzlements whatever. The big
three for you. If you remember it, your Glazer-Kennedy origin story.
How you got to us in the first place. Who, what, why and how you got
here. You guys want to start? We’ll go in that direction. It will pick you
up.
Jill: We’re Ron and Jill Wolforth from just outside of Houston, Texas.
We are businesses—where a series of businesses is in the baseball
pitching instructions. We have our own facility in Montgomery, Texas
called Ron Wolforth’s Baseball Ranch. It’s 20 acres in which we do not
only our local instructions but the bigger picture is our national
instruction. Where we have young men come in from all across the
country, as well as even some other countries, to come in and train in the
area of baseball pitching.
We are here because - how we’re trying to expand that now is into…
Dan: Can I clarify for a second?
Jill: Yes, sir.
Dan: Parents are the customer right? They’re writing the check?
Jill: That is correct.
Dan: Are they - is this motivated by my kid’s so good he’s going to turn
pro. He’s going to support me in the style to which I would like to be
accustomed? Is this motivated by — what’s driving the sale?
Ron: It is both. It is that and it’s also college scholarship.
Dan: Scholarship.
Ron: But it’s also a lot of wealthy parents that would like their kid to be
able to play a high school at their high school. You get all those different
levels.

Dan: Okay.
Jill: And in the competiveness the issue even becomes is my kid going to
make the baseball team in high school which is what Ron’s saying. A lot
of times it’s just trying to get an edge. When you always ask the question
what keeps them awake at night? A lot of times that’s what keeps them
awake at night. It’s making a team, it’s getting a college scholarship, it’s
being drafted, the whole realm. Obviously, people are willing to pay for
that if that gives their child an advantage on that.
Why we are here is that we’re expanding that into trying to now go even
more into private monthly membership clients that are willing to spend
more money to have us work with their athlete, again, all across the
country in a more detailed from just the 30 minute lesson that a local
person gets to being able to work with them regularly, paying a significant
amount of money. So that now we’re not only going into their pitching
but we’re dealing with their nutritional, we’re dealing with their mental
side. In essence building them as a complete athlete to give them a
distinctive advantage down the road.
How we initially…
Dan: I’m going to try and is it fair to call that adding a higher level on the
business? Is that…
Ron: Yes, it’s an additional, yeah.
Dan: Okay.
Jill: For example right now people will come into one of our what we call
our Elite Pitchers Boot Camps. They’ll pay $599 to come in for a three
day weekend. That’s their initial. That’s a — for these people that’s a
higher end already in that - in our industry.
Ron: In our industry not necessarily for an individual person.
Jill: In our industry, that’s a high end in our industry. Then of course
we’ve done continual back-end sales with those people. They’ll get the
products because not only do we do the instruction but we have a whole
information business on the instructions.
Side note for you with the hurricane that just went through. We were
actually hit by the hurricane even though we’re 100 miles off of the coast
line. One thing that we did is that we ran a Hurricane Ike reconstruction
sale. With our information products we went in at about two week’s time.

Dan: All right, gentleman.
Travis: I’m Travis Miller.
Jimmy: I’m Jimmy Vee.
Travis: We’re the authors of Gravitational Marketing here. We work
primarily in the automotive industry with both used car dealers and new
car dealers around the country and in Canada.
We run - we started out actually with the Standard Information product
that were sold for $1500. Got involved in a number of different other
projects and so it delayed actually making the next steps. Finally, after we
sold a couple hundred of those we said, “Gosh we ought to sale something
else to these folks.” We started the coaching program at about $500 a
month, which was nothing more than a conference call. Two conference
calls a month, one lecture style, one discussion or round table style. As
well as an implementation program focused around the ideas that’s
espoused in our system.
Jimmy: Basically, no one was using the system so we’ve built this
implementation program to actually walk them through the stuff they
already bought.
Travis: We of course began that program by selling back to the people
who had already invested in the system. Then replaced our—the front-end

-- 7 of 192 --

Marketing to the Affluent Transcript
Page 6
sale from being the system to being the coaching program which was a
dramatic shift in our business a few years ago.
In fact just this year we said as Dan says in the Affluent book here, “that
the Affluent are looking for life made easy, time saved and not to be
ripped off.”
We introduced some additional sort of done for you programs up to and
including full service agency work for some of the top end clients in the
group. Interestingly, we found that just through a hand full in just a small
percentage movement in the base was able to be 100% increase in the
business and maybe even a 200% increase in the net, which was dramatic
for us.
Let’s see, we ended up in the Kennedy organization, Glazer-Kennedy
organization, by way of Dave Dee and Harry Marshall, I guess. Through
various channels and then ultimately found the connections between them.
The common point of our — I would say lowest common denominator but
that sounds a little disparaging. Highest common denominator being Dan
here. We said, “Well wait a second we should get more involved there.”
Let’s see, the things that we’re looking for at least what I’m looking for,
I’ll let Jim speak for himself.
Jim: I think we pretty much knew the list out before we got here.
Travis: You may have thought of something different, is to increase
prices to our existing members. We’ve actually never changed the price
of our based level membership. There’s no reason we couldn’t have an
up-front fee involved. There’s no reason — you say in the book Dan, you
say that the people want it or they don’t, they’ve got their desire. The
price really isn’t much of a concern and that’s very true for our members.
When we’ve tried in the past to have it sort of going back to people who
haven’t bought with a lesser price offer, it really hasn’t been very
successful. It wasn’t the price that kept them from buying in most cases.
That translates to me to say if we were to increase the price the people
who are buying now would probably continue to buy.
Also to attract additional and more affluent members. I think that we saw
a big — when we introduce new programs, new done for you services, the
affluent immediately rise to the top, rose to the top. That represented a
significant change as I mentioned earlier.
We need to develop a new strategy specifically targeting the more affluent
in the bunch out of the roughly let’s say 50,000 automotive dealers in the
NOTES:
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 8 of 192 --

Marketing to the Affluent Transcript
Page 7
country between new and used that actually have any inventory or sell any
cars.
Then also sort of hallmark of our strategies that we teach them, I have to
honestly admit is actually to the dealers themselves is attracting lower-end
customers. Sort of everything we do is about attracting people who are
upside down in their trade or have bad credit. Of course that becomes
problematic these days. The banks aren’t advancing as much on the loans
so you can’t help them with the trade. They’re not loaning to people with
bad credit. The strategies start to get a little tenuous there.
One of the things that we’re thinking about doing is developing new
strategies or helping the dealers attract more affluent customers. I’d say
about 70% of our members are actually independent used car dealers.
We’re not talking about high line franchises like Lexus or Mercedes.
We’re talking about guys who have 30 used cars sitting in front…
Jim: This is the Tom Stanly buyer, the millionaire next door. The used
cars is the better buy. Luxury used cars are — can be sold as well, deep
discount.
Travis: That’s us, anything to add.
Jim: Before we started this we came from an automotive direct marketing
agency where we spent five and six years. Mostly working with mass
media radio advertising and moving these credit challenged buyers into
the accelerating them from not in the market to being in the market.
Which is different than what a lot of the two-step marketing, which is let’s
find the people who are interested and only market to them. You can
move those a lot easier than trying to educate someone to become a buyer
in the market.
We actually — using the radio we’re able to push, accelerate this market
— the market place and get non-contenders into the buying circle today
and get them into dealership in droves to buy cars. Generally cheap cars
or used cars where there was a lot of money to be made.
Obviously, that’s a shaky strategy these days. We want to create a new
strategy and basically divide our business up into a higher end concept
and…
Dan: Well you better create it quick. The issue in the niche. There’s a
number of niche guys who have gotten caught with having the equivalent
of not doing business with the top 5% who sell a lot of cars, then doing
business with the weak ones.
NOTES:
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 9 of 192 --

Marketing to the Affluent Transcript
Page 8
Many I’ve been nagging for years unsuccessfully. The guys of course
who are upside down first are the guys doing anything with the mortgage
industry and doing anything with the real estate industry.
They’ve taken the wrong hanging fruit which more often than not is the
weakest guys looking for life rafts and looking for magic pills and all that,
and not really going after, in real-estate, the $5 million producers or the
brokers only who actually have offices and equipment and aren’t going to
go quit and sell cars next week just on a whim. Same with the mortgage
guys. They’ve gotten caught. Had they three years ago made their
business increasingly about the best part of the niche rather than the worst
part of the niche, they’d have a lot less trouble today.
So you’re right in my opinion to break out if you will that part of the
business and be focusing on the most successful dealers. As you’ve
discovered, they are the most resistant to do anything. They’ll spend
money. They just don’t want to do anything.
All right, Chris.
Chris: Chris Heard. I had the commercial finance company that’s about
to become bank, National Bank. I’ll keep it out here. We’re having our
best year ever. I finance commercial property for business owners and
entrepreneurs all around the country.
It helps when most of your competition goes out of business as has been
lately. We’re very busy in that. There’s an interview with Dan and I in
the Affluent book about some of that.
I also am the IBA in Orlando where I have a pretty decent sized chapter. I
also have a coaching group, a local coaching group with about 15
entrepreneurs. I’m still not sure how Travis and Jimmy aren’t a part of it
yet, but we’ll work that out later.
Lately, because I’ve been talking about this message since the first of the
year about how in an economic softening if you will, there’s a lot of
assets, they’re at discounts out there. I’ve been talking about that in my
core business as a way to continue to motivate people to buy commercial
property for themselves because it’s a very easy way to increase their net
worth.
Actually did my — took my own advice and bought a franchise in August
at a deep discount. We’re actually rolling that out. It’s an upscale men’s
Barber franchise, membership driven. It’s been very successful in
Orlando for some time and we’ve actually — we were sort of launched

of money for us back then, 10 years ago. Obviously that was the start of a
lot of things. That’s me.

Bill: Bill Hammond. On my right is Matt Ziegula. Years ago I was
reading Jay Abraham, Gary Helbert, Dan Kennedy probably like so many
of you. After doing that for a pretty short period of time I pretty quickly
realized that Planet Dan was where I needed to be playing. It wasn’t
called Planet Dan back then.
I also pretty quickly realized that one of the cost of admission to the Dan
world is that as an attorney I would be constantly told what a mistake I
had made by attending law school. I couldn’t undo that. I just kind of
sucked it up and hung around. It’s been wonderful.
I am by training an elder law attorney and for those of you who don’t
know me. That’s means I do a lot of Medicaid planning, Alzheimer’s
planning. Those types of things.
I also pretty quickly under Dan’s tootaledge learned that it was time to
start learning how to teach others how to market their law practice, so I
coach attorney’s.
I’m here today, along with Matt, one of my business partners on this, for
something really pretty different thought. We have just started in the last
— it finally launched in the last week a social network. It’s the equivalent

and I have not been very conversant in it.

intimately involved in it have joined. We will now start marketing it.
We’re doing some research with Bob Regnarus and some other things.
The thing that I was hoping to get from this day in particular would be I

pretty much know how to do the information product side of that for

financial services for them. That’s Matt’s background.
In addition to that I’m thinking of something like, I haven’t seen it, but I
understand there’s a TV show called The Nanny where she goes in and
she fixes problem kids. You know parents have these kids that are acting
up and the nanny goes in. In a lot of ways someone who has a loved one
who has Alzheimer’s faces the same types of behavioral issues that
someone might have with children who are acting out.
I’m suspecting, although we haven’t done anything yet because we’re just
building this, that there could be a very high end business teaching care-
givers how to help and work with their loved ones who have the disease
and are acting out.
I can see a series of boot camps to train folks but I can also see very high-
end where we send someone in for a week or two weeks to train the
families on-site.
Anything along those lines is some of the types of things that we’re
hoping to get from this.

Other reason why I’m here is because I’m working with Millionaire

Right now the magazine is only bi-monthly. I’m exploring with the

each other.
I also want to switch that over into a membership network, Millionaire
Blueprints not just the magazine. Just kind of pump that up in terms of the
quality of the information we have, the type of people we have access to,
and the type of information that we could share.
I’m also looking at segmenting the audience because you’ve got young
people. You’ve got people who are already successful business owners
who love the brand and want more from it. Right now, I’m just exploring
different things there. I wanted to see what I can get out of this group just

— our clientele is mainly affluent. We’ve always catered to the higher

area these days with the automobile problems.
We are here because we relied a little too heavily on one marketing ploy

Marketing to the Affluent Transcript
Page 13
which is charity auctions. We got very good clients from charity auctions
for two or three years. Those people pulled back significantly in the area.
We’re looking for other ways to attract them, although we still get a good
business from them.
What we’re looking for is to expand our geographic range. We do have
clients in other states. We want to really capitalize on getting clients from
outside of our geographic area.
We also want to push more fully the product line of the paintings. We do
occasional portrait paintings that are a very significant price point and we
want to find out how to market more effectively.
How on earth did I find Dan Kennedy? I went to a Vic Johnson web —
developing Website in telemarketing. One of his freebies give away was
Magnetic Marketing. I started using that, trying it out. That was about a
year and a half ago, or two years I’m told now. Started playing with it.
There’s a GKIC group in Detroit which in southeast Michigan which I
joined in just gradually. Then I got the Marketing to the Affluent letter.
This was here, so here we are. Do you have anything to add, Nancy?

out and so that’s why we’re here.

Vision Day. Vision Day is a one-day strategic planning day either for
your life or for your business. There are four focal points for Vision Day
for the individual. The first one is I really want to discover what it is I’m
most passionate about. What my life purpose is about. What it is I’m
going to do when I grow up and we hear that a lot.

important to me. I know what I want to do with my life. I just want to
create something really big and important with it. I want to write a book
or make a movie or produce a Broadway play or start a company or create
a franchise or sell the company that I’m working in.

Dan: It’s very disturbing to people with a lot of qualifications that they
don’t matter much in the market place. They matter less and less and less.
I was doing a project for Gunthy-Renker. I don’t know a handful of years
ago. We’re going now to Iacocca’s house in Palm Springs. I got four of
the Gunthy-Renker kids with me who have been attempting to work with
him. Things have not been going well.
Greg would never have said it but one of the little pompous snots in the
car on the way over there said, “You know now, Mr. Iacocca asked us
about where we went to school and all that sort of thing.” He said, “I
understand you don’t have a college education. I don’t know if this is

problem now.” I said, “Probably the reason he’s asking you about your
qualifications is because you guys act like you don’t know anything.
That’s probably what’s sparking this particular problem.
As an aside, I’ll get to the main topic. There is one thing demonstrated
already here this morning. It’s not specific to Marketing to the Affluent,
although it has application there in the sense that ultimately we arrive at a
room of reasonably affluent people.
I don’t think there’s a single person who referenced the same point of
entry. There might have been two but I don’t think so. I think everybody
had a different point of entry. That’s not accidental. That happens on
purpose. I tolerate a fair amount of pain in order to be in a lot of places.
You didn’t even mention the photography coach by name whoever said,
who was it?
See, I don’t even know him. He’s second to somebody where I’m
probably John Hartman in your industry probably. But I don’t even know.
I do a lot to get articles out there to personally befriend some — like from
books you’ll get notes. I hand answer every one of them because you
never know when one of those people is going to be an influencer and you
can’t tell it.
The guy just sent me a book, The Chess Assassin’s Business Manual.
He’s some guy. He’s big in this niche. What do I know? He is the guru
to people who make a living either playing chess or coaching people on
how to play chess or running chess stores or running tournaments or
NOTES:
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 26 of 192 --

Marketing to the Affluent Transcript
Page 25
whatever. There’s five different places in his book where you find me
including pictures of covers of my books and websites.
Eight years ago he sent me a note. I wrote him back and that’s the end of
that. I sent him some stuff. I go to great trouble to try and be in all those
places, even though some of them are of small or unknown influence and
may only produce one or two, or three over time.
I think most marketers are too narrow in trying to get too much from one
place or one mechanism. They leave themselves vulnerable to disruption
for one thing. Secondly, they don’t achieve I call it omnipresence. When
you said work your way up the line to the last living guy, Terry. I mean
that’s — but I hear a version of that. I hear a version of that a lot. You
hear it from the smarter ones, which of course is who you actually want as
a client as well or a customer. I hear some version of that a lot is I saw
you mentioned, I heard you mentioned, by this guy. Saw that you had
influenced this guy and this guy and I decided screw it why not get to the
source. Why not move up stream as it were.
I call it omnipresence. My theory is if they see and hear about me in ten
different places then they’re more likely to move their way to me than
even if they heard about me a lot in one place. I think a lot of people just
miss this.
In the remaining time we have this morning let’s talk about the subject of
price suspension. Here’s the question, I have a few stories to tell you and
then you’re going to work. Here’s the question to be thinking about now
all the way through field trip and back. What factors totally take price out
of the equation where it’s not even an issue? This pretty much gets to
some version of something everybody came looking for. This is like the
question for the first half of the day.
A few stories. In your manual, I just showed it to you. You’ve got a page
right fairly yeah before the card. It looks like this. It’s the cover of a
magazine from a restaurant.
Anybody eat at El Molino, even in New York? You have? Okay. You
should go. A buddy of mine and I are in Atlantic City. Is this okay or did
I ruin it? Okay. We’re in Atlantic City the day — the first time the DOW
went below eight and it bounced around two or three times. It finally
crawled back up above eight. It pretty much was below eight. It was
Friday but two Friday’s ago I think.

El Malino which they opened one at Trump Taj Mahal. No 7:30 p.m., no
8:00 p.m. I had this 7:00 p.m. and there’s a 10:45 p.m. or something. I

take the 7:00. It’s 7:00 and there’s still a couple of tables, but by 7:30 the

the story is the bill’s $540.
The long driven story, there’s a reason for it and it’s not I mean because

stuff and they’re not interrupting your conversation. They don’t just tell

factor understand that makes price go away. They were knocking away

number one.
Story number two on my list to think about is this car. You have the full
color version in your book. You have his story incidentally typed out
about this car. This guy is 73 years old. He’s made a fortune, gone broke
three times during the time I’ve known him which is some 35 years, I
guess.
George started out in the pot and pan business, in the cookware business,
door to door. As many, many entrepreneurs, have. Invented the - now
this goes back to where cook ware was sold predominately to young
woman for their hope chest which you have to be of a certain age to even
know the concept. My father’s joke was it’s for young woman hoping
they’re going to have a chest.
That’s not - women used to create stuff before they got married, put it in a
big cedar chest in the attic and build up sort of the dowry of the
household. The cookware was sold to them as they were hoping to get
married. You sold mostly to high school seniors, their parents of course
picking up the tab and college girls.
George invented group selling to nurses. All young nurses of this
particular era hope to marry a doctor. That’s why - they were all there to
get married. They weren’t there to have a career. He invented group
selling cookware to nurses.
In a very short period of time he built a huge company. They had over
1,000 reps full-time all across the country. They were the only ones in the
cookware business ever to centralize appointment setting. All the - most
guys run two a night in direct sales. All the appointments were set by a
phone room corporate controlled which they were the only ones ever to do
this. Nobody’s done it since either.
George built an enormous business. Was carrying all of his own paper.
They were financing at 4% and when Jimmy Carter took office all of a
sudden all of the available credit was at 12 to 16%. That was the end of
that.
Shortly after, right after the bankruptcy what he had left – it’s really a cool
story. He had left no money. He had two cases of Think and Get Rich
books. They gave Think and Get Rich books to all of the reps and all the
dealers. He had four auto dialers because they used to use auto dialers.
Now we have voice broadcast now. Think of voice mail is - was a phone
machine, voice broadcast used to be an auto dialer machine. Some people
had rooms of them. You sort of manually programmed in the numbers
and then hit a button and the thing does work voice broadcast.

He went to real estate offices and sold the auto dialers and giving away
Think and Grow Rich books to sell them and discovered he could do that.
In about a year and a half he had the exclusive rights to import the number
one auto dialer. They had a huge company and 200 people in the phone
room selling auto dialers.
He was the largest importer and seller in auto dialing equipment in
America. The Federal Government outlawed auto dialing except for
politicians, churches and non-profits. So that company went from big to
nothing. That one he kept some money out of it.
The third business he built up over a period of time was a business
opportunity company. I’ve shown his ads from time to time. He had an
ad called how to make $4,000 a month at home with your computer. It
ran everywhere. From USA Today to Sewing Monthly to you name it. It
was right messa ge, right time. People were just kind of getting computers.
In fact for eight years – it was an $8900 sale and he gave them the
computer because not enough people had a PC. They got the box loaded
with 42 different businesses you could run on your computer for $8900.
They were building their own boxes.
That company hit $55 million a year in business. They got hit by the
Federal Trade Commission for deceptive claims. Mostly deal…
Dan: But anyway, some $50 million recovered. The FDC was relatively
kind. They let him keep half his money basically. They came and they
took half and they let him keep half. Half was still a big chunk of change.
The day after the FDC settlement he had a heart attack and was
pronounced dead at his house. He came back to life in the meat wagon.
Then he needed a transplant. Of course then he was — this was seven
years ago. That would be - he would be 67 years old.
His own doctors wouldn’t give him a transplant. They put him on like the
B list. They called him in one day and said, “You’re probably three years
away from getting a heart. You’ve got a year to live.” He said, “This
math doesn’t sound real good to me. I’ve got to go someplace I can get a
heart in 11 months or less. Where might that be?” Well there is no place.
When George tells the story he says so, everything he’s ever done he’s
gone to the library. He leaves the doctor’s office goes over to the library
and tells him his story. If I wanted to get a heart, where would I get a
heart? The librarian says, “Well here’s a list of all the hospitals in
America that do heart transplants that will take private pay patients.”

He went home and put together a nice direct mail package, pictures of his

which by the way happens all the time.
He informs them that he is going to be camped in their office until he gets
them telling everybody who comes in the door his story and that they
won’t give him his medical records because they don’t want to lose a $1
million heart operation, even though they’re not going to be able to do it.
After two days and calling the cops and the cops laughing at him they
relinquish and give him his medical records. Now he does his direct mail
campaign. He finds a hospital who will give him the heart. He’s had a
heart transplant now. He’s got it. It’s one nobody else wanted. It’s some
drug dealer who got killed in a police shoot-out. [Laughter] They warn
you that the thing can have problems. He’s been fine.
Since then now he spends his time kind of goofing around. He bought —
he owned for four years. He was one of — I think only eight private
owners in the country of a full-sized paddle wheel boat. One of these
Mark Twain three-story high ferry boat things and sailed around the
country. He’s ridden a horse from one end of the country to the other.
Now he’s — the last thing he did was bought himself this car. Rebuilt the
whole car from the ground up. Which I don’t know what it cost but it cost
some serious dough.
My guess would be he’s got $100,000 - $150,000 maybe in the car. I’m
just guessing. Clearly this is a guy for whom price is not particularly an
issue in anything that he decides to do.
The car is as good an illustration as any. He has an explanation in writing
for why the car. There’s another explanation as well. That is this is a guy
who misses competition. There’s this — this is a way to compete. You
take them to car shows and you win ribbons and people ooh and ah over
them and so forth. George has a reason to do things.

and change, $48,000 for a dental case. Case acceptance is about one out
of two. We would call that a closing rate in sales. They don’t call it that
in the profession but that’s what it is.
That average case is about six times the average in Richmond and three
times to four times the national average. There’s reasons why he gets it
and there’s reasons why people give it to him. Price is not — if you buy

Page 33
You’re buying the first edition that somebody else had that’s now
somehow passed its way back to them and is in decent condition. You
might spend $1000, you might spend $10,000, you might spend $100,000.
The decision clearly has nothing whatsoever to do with price. It can’t.
It’s a you either want it or you don’t want it. People want them based on
category. Somebody’s buying baseball books. Somebody else is buying
civil war history books. Somebody else is buying…
Those are stories to think about. You need to think about them in your
own business and hat you see in the sense of what makes these people buy
things where clearly the price on an objective basis is somewhere between
outrageous and obscene. Makes no sense. It’s unnecessary. There are
other alternatives at much lower prices readily available. What makes
them totally suspend any thought about price and go forward?
One key to it, and then I want to leave you to your devices for a few
minutes. One key, which I will give you is that everybody has at least
one, some people have more than one. It — sometimes it’s a lifetime one,
sometimes it changes. Everybody at every moment in time has at least
one thing that is their thing.
Now that may be something they collect. That may be something they do.
It may be something they’ve always wanted to do. It may be something
they are intensely worried and concerned about, frustrated about whatever.
Everybody has a thing. For one it might be their grandkids. For another it
might be their dog. For another it might be always having wanted this
precise car, not just collectable cars but this precise 1948 Lincoln.
Everybody has a thing. You do too for which price is totally suspended.
The trick is to find the people. They are eager to be found by the way. To
find the people for whom what you sell is or can be made their thing.
Because for them price is out of the equation in dealing with you. For
everybody else price is in the equation to one degree or another.
Now you have to hunt for clues. Like in your case see it’s not - it may be
in part whether they live in an area where it’s highly competitive to make
the high school team versus not so competitive to make the high school
team. There’s obviously income, household equity issues. Maybe sports
background in the primary parent is a psychographic issue versus not
having sports background. If dad played ball he’s more likely to be
whipped into a fervor by this than if he didn’t play ball.
I think my reaction never having played ball and having no sports
capability whatsoever would be I don’t really care if Billy wants to play
baseball or ballet. It don’t make any damn difference to me. Whereas if

www.getwsodownload.com

somebody played college ball and that’s a big thing. He’s got his trophies

suicide. Billy playing baseball is exactly what he wants.
You’re looking for this whole roster of things that you can use to get to
the person for whom your thing is at least at this moment in time their
thing or you have a way to make it so. Then price is completely out of the
question. Doesn’t make any difference.
For me it’s race horses, right. We just bought a yearling at a sale. The
trainer goes and says, “What do you want to spend?” I said, “Whatever
you spend. Whatever, just find what you think we should find and buy it.
If it’s $30,000 fine if it’s $50,000 fine, if it’s $80,000 fine. Just find the
best thing you can find down there and buy it.”
He’s frugal on my behalf. It wouldn’t matter, right. Everybody has this.

suspends price? We will get to a discussion of it before the day is out,
both in general and obviously specific to your business. What are the
things that make price disappear from the equation that can be helpful to
you?
Our first little trip, to Playhouse Square, you have to think about the fact

with price being fundamentally out of the equation. For one thing it’s
largely intangible. I mean you are getting better seats. You do get invited

For $25,000 a year a lot of people would say that value differential does

The value equation alone is not what makes that happen. The real
question is what does make it happen? Because it’s not a value
proposition. It is at the corporate level. They buy for other, but for the
individual it’s not that.

Really in almost all of these cases you don’t have the right value
proposition.

Italian restaurant at the opposite end of the resort. It’s not about that. And
all too often we try and support price simply by creating better value
propositions. Your logic will be what can we add to this that makes it
worth $10,000 instead of $5,000.

Marketing to the Affluent Transcript
Page 44
what your view as the mental things about the people you sell to that are
important.
My generic list about how they think differently starts with the fact like
we just said about the cars. That they are — the further up the ladder you
go the less needs-driven they are.
Chances are most of us don’t need to buy anything anytime soon. Toilet
paper maybe if you didn’t stock up, groceries maybe. Turns out as I said
the other day, my brother’s investment strategy looks better and better
with every day, which is buy toilet paper and beer in bulk. It’s outpacing
all three of my stock portfolios and my real estate selling.
Fundamentally we don’t have to buy anything. We are less and less
driven by need and function the further we go up the ladder. If you think
about all your business, nobody needs Vision Day. They managed to
muddle through last year without it. They’ll muddle through this year
without year. There are options. They probably own a Blackberry or a
day timer. Nobody needs to send their kid to you.
It is all about what we want to do, not what we need to do. That affects
how we think about everything. It’s already somewhat of a liberation
from value proposition and money by its inherent nature. If we’re not
buying by need and function it means we’re not buying by price. Those
are inextricably linked.
The buying of everything is more about the intangibles. As you said you
can buy theater tickets. You don’t need to buy that. You don’t need to
give her $25,000. It’s the intangible now about what that means to me,
which may be something different than what it means to him that is
driving that whole buying just decision.
Intangibles are often I think deeply psychological. There’s at least — if
we count the example I showed you with Douglas’s car, mine and now
Chris talking about getting an EMX. “A” we don’t need another car, and
we’re not buying them for any rational reason. What really are we
buying? Why are we buying these things? In at least two of our three
cases, I don’t know about you, but in at least in two of our three cases
we’re buying our youth, is really what we’re buying.
In Darren’s business one of the profound biases of the investors who are
buying Iowa real estate sight unseen is that they grew up in that area or at
least in the Midwest and left. They went either east or west to make all
their money. They’re not coming home. They’re at least sending their
money home. They are buying sort of a mental picture of even not what
was but what they recall what was, which they recall better than what it

These intangibles are about connecting with the psyche of the person or
with a group of people. It really is much liberated from the product or the
service that you are selling. No matter how hard we try and however
much we know it intellectually, it’s really hard to separate yourself from
trying to sell your damn product.
I think that’s one of the hardest parts of selling strategy, of crafting
messages because you live with the product every day. Try as we might,
we describe ourselves in connection to the product we sell. Sometimes in
connection to the market we serve. But still so probably in the room of
anybody this morning who comes close it would be Chris. Because Chris
commercial lending yada, yada for entrepreneurs of small to mid size
companies owner operator. He not only described the product, he
described the market in statistical demographic terms though not
emotional terms.
Still the product description is a big part of what we’re always focused on.
The great marketing breakthroughs are all about forgetting the product all
together. It’s really hard to achieve and yet again the greater the affluence
of the buyer the more important it is to achieve it because they are less and
less and less likely to buy a thing.

The fractional jet ownership business is never about the owning of 146th

there is none. I’ve thoroughly analyzed that. I don’t own. I buy charter
blocks of charter time. Either way there is no possible for anybody to
make that a value proposition. It’s impossible.
I make as much money per hour as 90% of anybody who makes a lot of
money per hour. I’m telling you, you can’t justify it. It just makes no
friggin sense whatsoever. You may be able to justify a trip here or there
but you can’t justify doing it on a continuous basis. You just can’t do it.

Dan: The good news is we don’t have to.
Male: Because they do it.
Dan: Well no, it doesn’t matter. There’s nobody I have to justify that to.

Male: Yeah, you’re justifying it yourself.
Dan: For some people that’s all about home with kids. Now it’s driven
by guilt, by what went on with their father when they were a child. It’s
driven by all that sort of stuff. For some it’s maybe a more pragmatic hold
marriage together thing. For some it’s symbolic but it is definitely not
about the thing.
I think this is the toughest thing to do is to get to the intangibles of what
we sell to the point that we are hardly thinking about or talking about the
thing we sell at all. They have more choices, obviously. More money
they got more choices they got. The less money you’ve got the fewer the
choices.
Poor people have no choices. They buy closest location, cheapest price,
who’ll give them credit. The choices are extraordinarily limited. As we
move up the economic ladder we get more and more and more varied
choices in every category of what we do.
If we weren’t having a meeting and we were coming to Cleveland for
some other purpose all of us in this room could choose stay anywhere that
Cleveland offers, from the best to the cheapest. Different ones of us might
make different choices at different times. Whether they were traveling

www.getwsodownload.com

-- 48 of 192 --

Marketing to the Affluent Transcript
Page 47
alone, traveling with spouse, traveling on business, traveling on
recreation. Coming here to go to the theater or coming here to go hiking
in the wild and look at llamas. Well hell we might as well stay at the
Comfort Inn because we’re going to be covered with crap anyway.
We have all the choices. The opposite end of the spectrum somebody’s
traveling and their only choice is either staying at their mother-in-law or
sleeping in the car. They have two choices.
This complicates our job thought as marketers because they have so many
choices. The good news is is that it’s not that all those choices are being
put in front of them. The higher up the affluence ladder you go less direct
outreach to them there is. In part because it doesn’t work as well. It’s
more difficult admittedly. It’s more expensive. Even more so because
people are intimidated about selling to them.
I thought that the more money I made the more I’d get pitched. That’s
what I thought when I started making decent money. I actually thought
one of the problems that would come with it would be being relentlessly
pursued and pitched and chased and having to deal with that. The
opposite has been true.
The non-profit sector tends to do more of it. Although I get less of that
than I would think. By the way I’m not inviting 25 pitches. Although
there’s actually little risk of even that happening because I’ve made this
point before and not been buried. Actually they get pitched less and less
but they know they have unlimited choices. It makes the tendency to
procrastinate on things that are not, that don’t have to be done or bought
or purchased right now even greater.
It takes more to cut through all that clutter and confusion. They tend to be
a little more deliberate about their purchases. They have the ability in any
category to do about anything they want. It’s important for us to know
that. They have the ability to go to just about anybody they want. They
don’t feel bound by geographic boundaries. A couple of you have
mentioned that this morning is something you wanted. It comes with the
territory. That’s the good news about it.
The more affluent the client, like she said taking people to New York from
here. They go there all the time. The problem with that Billy Elliot trip
by the way if they had a problem they didn’t really have a problem
because they filled it. The reason Carla and I didn’t go is we saw Billy
Elliot in London like the month before. I don’t want to see it twice. She
mentioned she had several other donors in the same position.
Putting that in front of everybody. Oh, we’ll take you to New York. You

We have to find a way in our category to make us once again their only

Page 49
The $10 million guy, he’s got as you say he’s got YPO, YEO, he’s got
Tiger 21 maybe that he could go be involved in. He’s got eight, ten,
twelve different executive coaching strategic planning folks that he’s
aware of. Now getting back to the position that we’re in a category of one
and we’re your only choice to get these wonderful outcomes you want is a
trickier task, but it is the task.
Now, your time issue. The first issue is particularly with their market, the
affluent entrepreneur’s self employee professional business owners but
really with many their time is of greater known value. The $50,000 a year
person really doesn’t put any value much on their time period. It’s a
problem to get them to do it. The typical small business owner doesn’t do
it, doesn’t do it well. It’s hard to get them to buy done for you services for
examples because they’re perfectly willing to do low value work and
mentally see no difference between it and doing high value work and so
forth.
You start to get up to high six figures and seven figures we have a real
sense of the value of our time. What the value of our time needs to be and
trade offs in dollar terms. We’re like conscious of it. We have a former
platinum member right now who’s attempting to develop a business that is
sort of a hybrid between entrepreneur mastermind groups and go on

don’t have four days. By the time I travel that’s six days. I don’t want to

more to the making of it. Here’s what this comes out to in my opinion in

that they, we, continue to do it with our investments even though we know

and again and again? Entertainers, athletes, wealthy executives, they turn

looking for.
When we finally find somebody we feel we can trust we just want to hand
the whole thing to them. I’m as guilty of it. I’m this guy. I got it split

of money to and not have to think about it. I don’t want to read anything.

need something I call him. Basically, he comes and fixes shit whether I

call them for me and find out. That’s what we’re more likely to do.

this month? We’re going to tell you what commercial to run this month

do like I do and don’t socialize. You’re not using it to prospect. You

I’m above taking the shuttle bus. It’s not that all. It’s that I can’t afford to
be seen arriving in the shuttle bus because people make decisions now
idiotically based on having seen me in the shuttle bus.
Try not to carry in boxes. Why? It’s not because I don’t mind carrying a
box. I’m not supposed to be seen. Sinatra had this great line. Sinatra
does not move pianos. Meaning I’m not rearranging the stage. It’s not
I’m above doing it it’s because. That guy, he’s got a status issue. Where
does he want to be seen? Where does he not want to be seen? What does
this say about me to others? What does this say about me to myself?
We’re selling to that. Emotional triggers are varied. One is, Chris you
said it, they deserve it. How do we justify things that make no rational
sense? In some respects we deserve it. We’ve sacrificed, we’ve worked
hard, we’ve whatever. We’ve made a big point of being born by the right
parent. That took a lot of time in research and effort. I don’t know
exactly how they justify it. Those of us who worked to get it we feel like
now we deserve the privilege.
Guilty. A lot of that. I know you guys got to deal with this in talking to
your folks. There’s relationship guilt. The kid guilt. My dad never made
more than x amount of dollars in a year stuff. The actual sort of deep
down belief that they actually aren’t worth $800 million a year to show up
and drive a company into the ground.
Male: How did you know that was the number?

-- 62 of 192 --

Marketing to the Affluent Transcript
Page 61
Dan: Frustrated resentful we’ve just talked about. Fearful, a lot of people
don’t think that the affluent are anxious, scared, fearful. They are. Their
mistakes have bigger impact financially and otherwise. Time is so
valuable so far. If we make the wrong mistake in choosing who we are
going to trust with something, not only did it maybe cost us money but
now we’ve got to spend some more time cleaning up the mess. We’ve got
to spend even some more time trying to find the next person that we’re
going to trust with this particular thing. Again the stakes are higher. We
have more anxiety about the decisions that we make. Then prideful,
whatever you wish to connect, ego, self-image etcetera.
Having said that now you guys tell me about the self made entrepreneur,
either the supplies what else supplies. What do you know about them and
how do you use it?

that story when I’m speaking. It sort of symbolizes that there’s sort of this
desire to do it. It’s because you’ve earned it, it’s time.

Dan: Have you guys watched the show The Mentalist? It’s on CBS. You
should watch it. It’s a detective show and there’s not much new that’s
worth a crap by the way. It’s not like you’re going to be missing
something on ABC or NBC by tuning in. I think its opposite Dancing
with the Stars and some other horrible…
The Mentalist, the premise is the one cop who annoys everyone else he
works with, is a former mind reader/con-artist/psychic guy. Now he is
using what he used to learn basically to defraud people of their money to -
in crime detection and in getting witnesses to tell him things and so forth.
Everybody thinks he’s reading minds but of course, he’s not. He’s using
psychology, manipulative psychology. They got it about — he’s very
good by the way. I don’t know the actors name but he’s very good at it.
They got it about right.
In one of the most effective things is people like someone who seems to
understand them without them having to tell them even their whole story.
Cold reading is all based on the premise of me being able to sit there and
say, gee I bet you feel like you’ve come up the hard way. It’s been a real
lonely road and there’s a lot of risk that nobody understands. the heavy
weight weighing on your shoulder. That would be a very effective
conversation.
It does translate into media. It does translate into print in speaking to that
same person. If you haven’t read anything about how to do cold readings
what the psychics call cold readings, you should. There’s some texts out
there. Go to Amazon and check under cold reading. There’s some good
stuff where there’s complete scripts, which are really pretty — the guys
that are really good at it are really phenomenal. They can pretty much sell
anything to anybody. It’s a very powerful skill.

-- 67 of 192 --

Marketing to the Affluent Transcript
Page 66
get to the trust thing.
What are some of the ways that we actually get to trust? Well one is we
get to trust through peer one recommendations. Someone we trust already
tells us you can trust this guy, this product, this service and this company.
That’s one way we get to.
The other way we get to trust is through sequential and repetitive contact
and communication. The old rule was no sex until the third date. Why?
No real reason, what’s the difference, first date, second date, third date,
why? The old joke about there is a guy that takes her out on a date. Gets
to the door kisses her. She goes inside. He rings the doorbell, she opens
the door and says number two. Because you’re trying to speed up the
process.
Everybody has without knowing it in their head a little ticker that says
after this person has done, has communicated with me x-number of times.
To have sex, maybe it’s three, to give him my money maybe it’s nine. I
don’t know what the number is for each person. There’s a point at which
there’s been enough communication, familiarity, reinforcement, proof
whatever that now there’s sufficient trust to take this step.
Playhouse Square starts with an advantage because they got physicality.
They’ve been around since 1812. Pretty much anybody’s who’s a
Clevelander, even if they’ve never been there, knows kind of what they
are and that they’re there. They start, now if she was starting from scratch
and had drawings of what was going to be there on easels in a tent she’d
have a tougher time getting $25,000 out of me based on two years from
now you will be able to have a good seat to see the Lion King. That
would be a tougher job.
Part of the answer to this is to try and control the gestation process from
early to end and to accelerate it as best you can. One of the things the
RAHM guy doesn’t do is he doesn’t do anything.

to speed with repetition. This is to be simplistic its number of touches.
If I’m going to try a move a CEO. I had a client years ago who sells
basically, strategic planning to CEOs. Follows elegantly and intelligently
than you, nevertheless that’s really, what he’s selling. He would get some
CEOs to raise their hand. Then he would send them this very good
newsletter. That basically was his system. Send them the newsletter
every month until they cracked. That basically was his system.
I said, “All right, what happens if we send it to them twice a month?” I
don’t know. I said, “Well right now it’s taking you two years to get
somebody to crack. We send it to them twice a month maybe we can get
them to crack in a year.” Not true, but got it to 18 months doubled
frequency but not double acceleration, but a 50% increase in acceleration.
Male: Did you send him the same newsletter two times?
Dan: Yeah, every issue was different.
Male: You published the newsletter twice a month instead of once a
month.

Website.
I have more but here’s what’s really cool about that. They have cut off
the, I need to ask you 56 questions before I give you money. That’s what
they’ve done. They’ve moved it behind the first movement of money.
They basically said you don’t need to have your questions answered until
you do this. Then you get to look at all the details and know where all the
properties are and how many square feet they are and do they have bunk
beds and don’t they have bunk beds. They’ve moved this back after a step
of giving money. That’s what’s really cool at what they’ve done.
Next step due diligence. Your membership director will then assist in
providing you with more detailed information and guide you through the
remainder of the enrollment process. Meetings will be scheduled to
include an asset review, a club review and a membership document
review.
We’re going to explain what these are. Club document review, your
membership director will help orient you with the layout of the club
membership portfolio and will answer any question you may have. At this
stage, we strongly encourage you to begin calling existing members to
hear first hand the benefits and experience of membership.
Let me stop there for a second. Here’s what they’ve done with this. The
first thing a bunch of nimrods wants to do is call the existing clients.
Which is a big pain in the butt because “A” they’ve got stuff to do,
somebody gave them $450,000 does not necessarily want to be working
for them for free.
If they pay them, they’ve got to disclose it. Really, they can’t afford to
pay him enough to justify. They’ve got to back that up to the point that
nobody wants to do it anymore. They have solved their problem by
creating this process and literally saying we’re going to encourage you to
call a bunch of people, just not yet at this particular point in time. By the
time they get to this point in time, hardly anybody feels the need to pick
up the phone and make the call. That’s how they’ve solved this problem.
Asset review, an asset review will be scheduled with and your spouse or
life partner to discuss in detail all of the assets of the club, residence of
sail boats, city clubs, automobiles, tours and jets.
Next, the club review. During the club review your membership director
will discuss with you any and all questions you may have regarding our
company profitability capacity model, future direction of the company
etcetera.
NOTES:
www.getwsodownload.com

Membership documents. Final documents are sent to you for review and
signatures. Finally, new member orientation. A senior associate of our
team will meet with you to cover all aspect of member’s services, policy,
procedures and the reservation process.
Backing up again. At the asset review stage where they’re going over all
of the details of all the properties, the sailboats, the cars, the trips, the
tours. Your spouse or life partner has to be on the phone for that meeting.
Why? Because you’re not getting out after she hears abut the residences,
the sailboats, the city clubs and the automobiles and the tours.
Male: I’ve got to talk it over with my spouse either.
Dan: None of that either, that’s correct. They have exercised an
enormous amount of control in the order in which people make a decision.
They have solved a lot of their problems that would normally occur by
doing so but made the solutions palatable to everybody. It’s pretty sharp.
It’s pretty clever.
Male: Also you know it’s, they’ve built in a time commitment to every
step which is one thing when I have executives where we want to develop
trust is to make these small, start again at the beginning.
Dan: Yeah, if you don’t mind.
Male: From what I heard, they built a time commitment into every step
both on their part and on the part of the perspective buyer as one way of
developing trust, which we talked about before. Having those concrete
discrete time, cycle times built in is going to engender trust right at the
beginning. Hopefully they don’t miss the deadline.
Dan: Right. Also what it does is they’re not in a hurry. You relax about
being sold because they’re not trying to close you right away. They don’t
appear to be worried about you getting away, which is something we often
screw up a lot.
Male: Were those guidelines, the approval process included with the
initial?
Dan: Right, came right in the initial package, yep. When you respond to
lead generation advertising at this time, this is a year ago or so, this is
what you get.
Now it was in a nice package and the DVD was not falling out all over the
place. It got beaten up a little. Yeah, that’s right in there from the
beginning.

want to obviously have an above average profitability for franchisees.
One of the keys was the simpler the concept the better. Preferably, that
probably meant no food. If you’ve ever looked into food franchises
there’s all sorts of spoilage issues. There’s all sorts of health regulation
issues and whatnot. We wanted to have something fairly simple that
anybody could do, whether it be for an absentee owner or second business
or what have. Obviously, that spreads the fields in terms of who can buy.
The other thing we wanted something is ideally would be niched
particularly to as Dan always says those least and last effected by
recession. One of the things that we want to do is ideally deal with
somebody that’s a necessity. Well some of the stuff that you’ll see here is

approximately 2 point-some odd billion men have hair on their heads.
Probably getting haircuts regularly. About three billion men are growing

an up-scale men’s barber club essentially.
We’re taking what normally is for most guys is not a real pleasurable
experience. It’s a necessity. A lot of men that I know, as I’ve been poling
them lately for the last nine months or so, they’ll go to the Hair Cuttery,
the Great Clips, Super Cuts whatever. It’s more out of necessity as
opposed to something they enjoy. They’ve got to go once every three or
four weeks that’s what they do. Or maybe they, if they’re particularly
vain maybe they’ll go to the same salons their wife goes to or some other
thing like that.
Not a lot of guys can find the independent barber that maybe we grew up
with or maybe your father went to for instance. This kind of taps into a
nostalgic element that I think is for most people as Dan was saying earlier
sometimes your fake memories are better than the real memories. A lot of
times I think this is one of those concepts.
For a lot of people we have a vivid memory of perhaps the first time
watching your father shave or putting on shaving cream or something to
that effect. It’s - and if you can create an environment that’s more like a
sanctuary, which is really what we’re trying to create with this. It’s a
place where guys will go relax pamper themselves a little bit, indulge in
an affordable luxury. That’s really how we’re trying to pitch this. There’s
a lot of stuff here.

tri-fold brochure. There’s some stuff in here from my newsletter.
The last page that kind of goes with all of this is Dan I don’t think you

to create something that would really wow everybody.
What we did was you all recognize that? I-phone box. It’s black, kind of a

much larger obviously. In there is a straight razor kind of shaped like an
a-pec so it’s opened with a label on there, shaving brush the same thing.
There’s an engraved piece in the middle.
Of course what we’re trying to do is obligate a future franchisee that and
these don’t cost much. You can literally go to the mall engraving places
and put their name on this little thing in the middle. It’s not a big deal.
Then there’s all the copy and little booklet. There’s the financial
disclosure document underneath. It’s this - it’s a box that’s probably
going to be about I don’t know what that is, 18 inches in length, probably
12 to 15 inches in height and probably four or five inches deep.
It’s going to cost some money but it’s going to make kind of an interesting
impact I think to people. Especially that we’ve already - we’re only going
to give it to them after we’ve kind of vetted them through a process a little
bit and we’re pretty sure that they’re interested in buying a franchise or a
master.
My point back to Dan’s points he wanted me to cover is this isn’t - this
really is an experience business. I talk a lot about in there and we don’t
have time to go into all of the details. I talk a lot about in there borrowing
some concepts from Starbucks. The Starbuck’s they originally did it it
was meant to be. They talk about the third place all the time between
home and work. Great Britain they have the pubs well in the black
community here we have barbershops. We don’t really have that in more
of the global professional community. That’s what we’re trying to achieve
here with this. It’s all about the experience.
You walk in, it’s the same type of atmosphere no matter which one you go
to. Hardwood floors, white wainscoting about half way up the way. The
old-fashioned barber chairs. You sit down. It’s generally if you’re getting
a haircut and a shave. It brings back the nostalgic straight razor shaves,
which is kind of a lost art to begin with. Everybody’s a licensed barber.
The way the engage with you is meant to solicit whether you’re in the
mood to talk or not talk. If there’s anything that the guys in the room
NOTES:
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 87 of 192 --

Marketing to the Affluent Transcript
Page 86
probably know if you’ve gone into one of the chop shops and you get a 20
year old cosmetology student and wants to know what number your blade
and all this other crap. The next thing you know she’s telling you about
her boyfriend problems and her kid problems and all the other stuff that a
lot of guys just really don’t care about for the most part. They just want to
go there and sort of zone out for a while.
This provides that type of atmosphere. Particularly when you get to the
straight razor shaves, which most people do because they can fall asleep in
the middle of it. What’s interesting to know about this is we’ve built this.
It’s a membership model that has a very high-perceived value. Candidly,
depending on what membership level you’re at, and there’s four different
membership levels. The very high end, which is $120 a month all the way
down to the bottom end at $40 a month, which gives you unlimited
haircuts.
In between is where most people pick. What’s interesting is you get
unlimited haircuts, at certain levels you get unlimited shaves. When you
walk in you’re offered water, soft drink, beer, coffee. The beer is at the
moment it’s only Samuel Adams we’re actually working on our own
private label beer, which is a little bit easier than I thought I would be.
The whole experience is effectively choreographed to be a guy’s place.
Where they go in, they relax. They go get the service that they need to.
It’s really more about it’s about belonging to this club. That’s really
where we’re going with this.
We think most of them can probably have about 400 to 550 units, oh
excuse me members. I just bought one that I was a member at for over 2
½ years which got me involved in this whole thing. Not because I needed
another business because I don’t. I’ve got somebody that I’ve hired which
happens to be the wife of one of our other management team members.
The other two guys on management team just bought the master for
Tampa. All of us are actually really excited about this concept and believe
in it enough that we’re actually involved in it, putting our own money into
it at this point.
I told you earlier about some of the — we’ve had a lot of pretty good
success pretty quickly without really fully blowing out the doors in terms
of the marketing just yet. My point is again it’s back to we’re not selling
haircuts. We’re selling a place that any professional guy would want to
belong to. And it just so happens that when he’s there. He gets the beer.
He gets his haircuts. He gets a straight razor shave. He gets treated the
correct way. There’s a little bit of a scalp and shoulder massage thing at
the end. And all sorts of stuff like that.

far from my house. I was excited when I saw that was in my general area.

to drop in just a men’s barbershop in a strip plaza. This isn’t some high-

didn’t try and sell the cut at all. They wanted to sell the membership.

unlimited haircuts and shaves was only $55 a month.
It’s basically just a couple of dollars more than buying one a la carte. You
can get the unlimited. Basically, it turned out to be that by pricing the a la
carte so high the membership seemed like a bargain. Then you starting
going wait, I’m spending $55 a month. Normally I spend $18 a month on

steal, like a bargain. I felt like I was getting the better of them. I’m going
to come in here every week and get a shave and haircut.

their credit cards right at the gate. Those cash flow ebb and flows go
away pretty quick.

What happens is I’ve got eight or ten people playing poker at the table
everybody is walking by and they got probably eight to ten people

going to join this membership? It works pretty well.
Dan: From a business standpoint, there’s a lot to like about it in terms of
franchising. What’s important I think from a marketing standpoint is it
gets to this concept of what is a business really all about. As Chris said,
this business is not about haircuts. Because you can go over to no
appointment haircutters and if you don’t make the stink of perm solution
and you don’t mind woman talking around you about their hysterectomy
and you don’t. You can get in and out of there and get a haircut.
This is not about that. This is about a compositive ingredients, which is
the other point that I would make to you. The other example that I usually
use is the World Wrestling Federation, which is part comic book, and
comic book heroes, part soap opera, because they have continuing
storylines. Part rock concert because the environment is very much
designed to evoke the same physiological response and emotional
response to being at a large rock concert.
They took pieces from businesses and stitched them together. Here
there’s a little Starbuck’s. There’s a little ole men’s club that pretty much
doesn’t exist anymore.
Dan: There’s the physical environment itself within a small footprint.
You walk in and you know you are in. You’re not in no appointment
haircutters for one thing. You are in an upscale but not so upscale that
somebody might be uncomfortable, but an upscale male place.
By the way each store, each shop, what language are we using store, shop,
club? Each club, forgive me. Each club may have certain designated
times when dad can bring son or grandson. We’ve eliminated them being
there whenever they want to be there.
You can look at your monthly calendar as a member and know Saturday
afternoon from such and such to such and such and the third Thursday
night that’s dad’s and granddad’s night. If you have somebody you want
to bring for that experience that’s great. If you don’t want to be exposed
to kids. You’ve just got to avoid those times if you want the quiet
sanctuary is a very good word. Things are being avoided. There’s a
sports clips thing. I almost bought the master rights to Ohio a few years
ago for that thing.

Ironically, Playboy is, not so much now, but was built on much of the
same concept. Playboy was a business about something. Superficially it’s
about naked woman but that’s not at all what the thing was ever about.

paying attention to. They built a culturally driven, concept driven
business that was really much like what you guys talked about the Greg
Norman Land Rover ad.
It was about identifying with this type of person and this lifestyle more so
than it really was anything else. They did it brilliantly. Had they had a
theme park then that would have been Ritz Carlton, Four Seasons. It’s not
just value proposition. It is people. It’s a business about something that
people identify with. Top flight kind of elite exclusive service, being
pampered, everything taken care of.
If you look in the restaurant world, so you know what’s the difference
between Outback and Taco Bell. Well Outback to the mass affluent and
slightly down mostly sells experience. Outback is go to Outback tonight.
Here we are having fun with all of our friends. They’re running a product
commercial right now but that’s kind of unusual for them. They usually
don’t run product commercials. They usually run happy experienced
commercials and oh, by the way you eat.
Taco bell is a product ad driven company. It’s always the next new type
of taco, the next new type of burrito. It really is about how much food can
