-- 96 of 192 --

Marketing to the Affluent Transcript
Page 95
you get for how small amount of money. That’s really their deal.
Male: Getting full.
Dan: That’s right it’s about get full, for $3. We’ve seen now Subway’s
made a terrible mistake deviating from what was working for them and
going to the how full can you get for $5 approach which has put them
right back as an ordinary fast food entity competing with other, very
dumb.
The low end and the middle end of the restaurant industries reacted very
stupidly to the recession. The high end is still behaving intelligently. The
middle and the low are behaving very, very stupidly.
Outback is really about something that has some leverage. Taco Bell is
about something that has no leverage whatsoever. It’s very easy to beat
them. Just fill people up for $2 instead of filling them up for $3. You will
beat them. That’s the last kind of business you ever want to be in.
We’ve talked about Cadillac. Cadillac used to be about having arrived.
They’ve lost their way and trying to figure out now what they’re about.
You can tell if you watch five Cadillac commercials it’s what we were
complaining about McCain somebody this morning. If you watch five
Cadillac commercials, all five of them have are about something different.
There’s not a consistent thing that Cadillac ownership is about. There
needs to be.
One of the things to think a lot about is what your business is really about.
There’s what your customers even consciously think it is and yet what
they subconsciously respond to about it. What it is to them. You want a
business that is very, very clearly cultural and concept driven that people
identify with and connect with and want to be a part of and feel like
they’re a part of. You don’t want a business that is transactional between
provider and consumer.
Male: Just a quick thing about language if you want to use language in
your copy edit promotions, community versus family versus what…
Dan: Well first of all, it all matters. That’s why somebody asked her this
mor - oh Marlene asked her about script. It all matters. One word or
another may not matter but eight pages of word choices aggregate, 15
minutes of word choices aggregate, matter. The overwhelming majority
of sales people as well as those who use media are way too casual about
this. Way too thoughtless, as if one is the same as another.
Community, family, network, association, they have very different
NOTES:
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 97 of 192 --

Marketing to the Affluent Transcript
Page 96
meanings to different people. People like read or hear on multiple levels
at the same time. If you make them read something aloud they realize
they’re getting something different from it than what they thought they
were getting from it.
There’s an overall impact that occurs to somebody from what you say to
them or what you say to them through print or other media that is not line
by line by line or word by word by word but the aggregate impact of all
those choices.
For one group or purpose, community might be perfect. For another it
might be the worse of any of the possible choices. Sometimes we wind up
using - so I take online community or take a phrase like social networking.
We’re using now a, really a technical term, a convenient term that
describes a particular thing. We’re using it unilaterally and universally
with everybody.
To some people the idea that it communicates, oh boy I get to be a part of
a community. I don’t have to leave the house. I don’t have to leave the
office that sounds great to me. To somebody else that sounds like the
worst thing that you could ever ask them to be apart of. If you rephrased
it they would want it. I think we have to be very, very careful. The more
you do one message for all, the more trouble you’re in.
When somebody earlier said well you guys said yeah but that would
require work so that rules it. Well yeah, and that’s the case here. Every
business attempts to get by with a single message to multiple
constituencies.
Some media I guess you have no choice, if we’re going to do a TV
commercial. We don’t have a lot of choices. We have geographic if
we’re not running nationally. If we’re going to do cable we have
conservative political. We have really conservative political. We have a
different demographic at the Cooking Channel than at the Hunting and
Fishing Channel.
For the most part if you look at most businesses this is what they do.
They have different constituencies of customers and they have a single
message. They sacrifice an enormous amount of effectiveness by doing
this as opposed to cutting, really running multiple vertical businesses
under one umbrella and matching a constituency into a business.
You have, you can sell your thing to what, somebody with a $1 million a
year business to a $10 million a year business. You start to get into the
$10 million the guy thinks of himself as a CEO. At $1 million he doesn’t
think of himself as a CEO. There’s a difference between somebody who
NOTES:
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 98 of 192 --

Marketing to the Affluent Transcript
Page 97
thinks of themselves as a small business owner and somebody who thinks
of himself as an entrepreneur.
All this language now matters with each one of those people. See like
here she’s right about this morning. There was such a pronounced east
sider, west sider thing that you might as well be talking about two
different planets. The only thing that unites them actually is the Browns.
Otherwise, you might as well have the Grand Canyon between the two
sides.
They have a different language. They view themselves as totally as
different tribes, and essentially they view each other with distrust and
disdain. It hasn’t changed since I grew up here. Generations are not made
a lick a difference.
If you’re going to run one big ad in the Cleveland Plain Dealer you are,
you either are going to make it for one or the other or sacrifices the other
or you dilute the entire thing. If you go into one of those community
newspapers now you can tweak it for those two sides. You’ll do better. I
don’t think people give a lot of sensitivity to this at all. If you come at it
from a copyright - copywriter’s good ones spend a lot of time on word
choice. That tends to train us a little bit to do it verbally as well.
Sales people who become copywriters often don’t have that sensitivity to
it at all. It makes where we’re going for dinner, like for example I heard
them in training meetings make this major issue about never using the
words “Chef’s specials.” At the meeting that I was at she patiently
explained at Denny’s they have specials. We have $35 lunches. We don’t
have specials. We have Chef’s features.
In of itself does that affect anybody? No. The aggregate of how they
described that. How they described the wine. How they described the
way the salmon is prepared. The aggregate of all that does have enormous
impact. As you’ll hear it probably described, they’re pretty tough about it.
Their manager’s got to go ape manure when they hear the wrong language
being used. They’re pretty sensitive to it and they’re paying attention to it
very, very closely.
Their premise is by the way that they’re not in the restaurant business.
Their premise is they’re in the relationship business between the wait staff
and the customer. His whole premise is anybody can put up a great
building and find a chef and serve great food. That’s not what keeps them
coming back. They have to have a great relationship with Helen. Helen’s
got to be superb. We’ve got to invest all of our time, energy, effort and
money in making Helen great.

Dan: Let’s talk about - what I’m going to try and do is talk a little bit this
morning about both direct outreach and referrals. Then we have our trip
to the dog resort and lunch when we get back. Then I know there’s a few
people who are eager to do show and tells and give people things and tell
them about what they do. We will accommodate that. We will probably
spend the rest of afternoon literally just doing question and answer
discussion and do our best to knock everything off that was on the list.
Let’s start with going direct to the affluent. The first thing you should
know is that everything’s the same. It’s just admittedly harder. There is
fundamentally no process differences.
Fundamentally there’s always only two ways that we can find our way to
a buyer of whatever it is that we sell. We can either get a list of those
NOTES:
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 100 of 192 --

Marketing to the Affluent Transcript
Page 99
buyers or high probability buyers from someone else or we can create a
list from scratch. That doesn’t matter whether we’re selling to the poorest
person on the planet or the richest person on the planet. Process is exactly
the same, and really there’s only two options. There’s all sorts of little
mini options within the subsets but there’s only two options.
In essence we’re either going to generate leads through advertising online,
offline, direct mail, exhibiting in a show, putting a radio thing on whatever
it is that we’re going to do. We’re going to generate leads. We’re going
to get somebody to raise their hand. Or we’re going to buy, borrow or
rent leads from somebody who’s already done a lot of that work and
compiled a bunch of them in one place that seemed to fit our parameters
of who our idea client is.
By the way, it is critically important — one of the most difficult things to
do and one of the things nobody spends enough time on initially or
continuously is getting clearer, and clearer, and clearer, and more
complete, and more complete, and more complete about who is my
customer or client. The more you know about that, the more efficient and
better that you can be.
I’ll give you an example. Right now I’m experimenting, launching a new
marketing newsletter just for doctors. Crossing the professions, chiro,
dentists, optometrists, veterinary, podiatrists whatever. Much as I did in
1979. I’m going to pass the mail piece. I have a lead generation direct
mail piece. Those are compiled list. You would rent all the chiropractors.
You would rent all the dentists.
The selects available. You can pick states. You can pick zip codes. In
some cases you can pick years in practice or number of employees. That’s
like about it. The way most people then would do this is they would have
that database electronically sent to a mailing list house. It would mail
them all. That would be that.
In this case, I can tell you empirically that anybody doing that would be
out of the game in a very short period of time. It’s not enough selection. I
am sitting on my couch with 25,000 labels of dentists. First of all, peeling
off the dupes because every list has dirt in it. The same name said two
different ways. Husband and wife at the same office. Ultimately yes, you
want to hit them all but in testing you don’t.
I’m peeling off all of the unpronounceable Islamic and east Indian names.
I know empirically they’re not good customers for me. They never have
been. They never will be. Dr. Ishamad Pachubaba, he’s going to get a
mail piece from if I stay in this game somewhere around 2028. [Laughter]
Right now, he’s coming off. Every woman with a hyphenated name
NOTES:
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 101 of 192 --

Marketing to the Affluent Transcript
Page 100
coming off. The probabilities of them buying are much lower, much
lower than a woman without a hyphenated name. On and on and on.
Out of those 25,000 labels we’ll mail about 18,000 of them. The rest of
them will be stuck on my knee in big wads and carried over to the trash
bin. Nobody else can do it. I can’t delegate it because I knows it when I
sees it. I’ve been at it for 30 years. I can look at 20 of them labels and I
can tell you those two guys aren’t going to buy from me. To have you sit
there and do it by the time I explain it all to you I could actually have gone
and suffered through doing it while I watched Dancing with the Stars.
You may or may not like that piece of information. I will tell you it’s one
of the ways people shoot themselves in the foot. Me doing it this way I’ll
barely eek out 1%. If you do it the way most people will do it. They’ll be
down around a fourth of a percent to a half a percent. The difference will
be being able to stay in the game or not stay in the game.
Eventually, I’ll know a lot more. I’ll know first ring of suburbs versus
second ring of suburbs versus real small town. What about 20 dentists in
the same building with different suite numbers? Do I get more than one of
them or do I wind up with one of them every time? If I wind up with one
of them every time then we never mail to more than one of them at a time.
What about married or not married? What about, so eventually I’ll know
all sorts of things which will keep narrowing the universe of who I go
back to with the second and the third and the 15th and the 18th and the 27th
attempt. I see you.
The more you know the better it gets. Early on whenever you’re starting
your results are always going to be skewed bad because you don’t have
good information. Then your results are going to improve as you get better
and better and better and better and better information.
Male: If you’re looking at the information.
Dan: If you’re looking, if you’re looking at the information. Yes. If you
take a dentist, when I’ll serve you about $3 million in two years. We
initially didn’t know anything about who would respond to this idea of
owning a weekend dental assistance school that made money in your
practice on a Saturday. We don’t know, so we mail everybody.
Gradually over a few months you discover, both from looking at the data
and listening to what they are telling you, we discovered “A” the
overwhelming majority of them were 55 years of age and up and viewing
themselves as ten years away from retirement in the practice and knew
they didn’t have enough money in order to quit. We knew they all came
from the second and third rings around the city not from in the city.

In Georgia for example that now means anybody that’s in Atlanta doesn’t
get any mail. It’s everybody in the ring around Atlanta that we want to
mail to. Given that information after about 90 days, “A” it affects who
you select to communicate with, and it affects the message dramatically.
The primary reason they were buying was not anything we were even
saying at all in the initial communication. They on their own had to figure
out, “Well shit, if I buy this and I can eek an extra $100,000 a year out of
it. I bank the $100,000 a year,” which of course we all know they won’t
but they think they will.
“If I bank the extra $100,000 a year. I do that for the next 10 years I’ll
have $1 million I don’t have now which will take care of the short fall in
my retirement account because of all the money I wasted.”
I have two things I can sell back to your real-estate in your story. I have
two things I can sell instead of one. I can sell the practice and I can keep
the school. I can sell the school, keep practice. I can put the two together
and sell them both to some guy.
They’re figuring all of that out on their own and telling us. Now not only
does it affect who we go to. It changes what we say. Now we say that.
For everyone smart enough to figure it out on their own there’s 20 who
aren’t smart enough to figure that out on their own.
When she says yesterday well they don’t read the stuff we send them.
What she doesn’t know is the primary reason people don’t read the stuff
you send them is that it doesn’t leap right out and connect with what’s
already important to them. The how your principle of the conversation
already going on in their minds. These docs what are they — the thing
they’re thinking about is I want to retire. I want to get the hell out of this.
I don’t have enough money. Oh my God, I’m not going to have enough
money in five years. I’m not going to have enough money in ten years.
They all went and sat down with their standard off the shelf financial
planner. Who used his standard off the shelf software program to put a –
print out the little chart of how much money you have to have in order,
with inflation and all that shit, in order to live the way you want to live.
They thought they were going to need $1 million. The financial planner
just showed them they need $8.6 million. Now they’re in despair. That’s
what they’re thinking about every day.

They will spend a lot more money talking to the entrepreneurial parent

that the grandparent actually has more influence in this than a lot of
people would think. They have a multiplied opportunity with the
grandparent. The parent has two. The grandparent has an average of five.
If you talk to the grandparent you have five prospects. If you talk to the
parent you only have two prospects. Kind of interesting.
My point is most people don’t do well with direct outreach really at any
socio economic level. Particularly at the affluent level where it is harder
it’s more difficult to start with. They don’t do well because they’re
shooting blanks and the cost of all the waste eats them alive.
If you’re going to do it you’ve got to be continually working at more and

right now who are dentists, who are women, who have hyphenated last
names? I’m sure we do.

without than the ones with. I can’t tell you why that is. I’m not
suggesting it’s true of everything you sell by the way. I’m just telling you
it’s true for a practiced marketing information.

you talking. With — if you have a really good profile of who your client

example, let’s say you know a lot of shit that it’s really hard to find it
anywhere.
The lead generation ad is now all about what you know. Now you have an
ad running in Investor’s Business Daily that says, “Attention grandparents
or great grandparents with net worths exceeding $5 million who are
profoundly concerned with family and legacy and take group family
vacations at least three times per year, play golf twice a week and wear
toupees. I have information specifically for you that you need to see
before you make any decision whatsoever about x, y or z. Go here and get

I did that clumsily, not elegantly, but you get the idea.
Female: Well ask them for a response [inaudible]
Dan: Yes, yes. Not in the kind of numbers that non-affluent people
would respond to an ad like that. Therefore the cost of each response is
quite high. Your economics of your business have to allow for them. The
value of them has to be such. Your follow up on them has to be. What
happens in every business is — the first part of your question. I can
generate a lead for anything, from anybody, any group of people. The
only question is what it’s going to cost me to generate the lead. I can get
somebody. I can get that person to raise their hand.
If you go all the way up and you tell me you want billionaires. You want
a billionaire who’s concerned with whatever. There’s media for me to go
to where I can run a lead generation ad. I can get a damn billionaire to
call my 800 number and request information. It’s going to be pricey. I
don’t know how pricey but it’s going to be pricey. But I can get it done.
What almost inevitably happens is everybody looks at the number of what
it cost to make that happen and throws their hands up in the air and walks
away. That’s good news for the few smart ones who react very differently
which is, okay here’s the number. Now we know over time if we get
smarter we can make the number better. Our first challenge is to figure
out how in the hell we can live with the number. Not oh, my God the
number’s horrible. How can we live with the number?
NOTES:
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 106 of 192 --

Marketing to the Affluent Transcript
Page 105
That’s really how businesses are built. On one hand now you’re
constantly trying to improve the number, mitigate the number, help the
number any way you can. On the other hand, you’re trying to live with
the number.
In many scenarios if you figure out how to live with a “bad” number you
almost never have to worry about anybody figuring it out to compete with
you. They won’t. They just flat won’t.
There’s a guy — you guys know anything about the sports tout business?
He probably does. Sports touts are —d o you know who Jim Kramer is on
CNBC? He gives you stock picks. He’s equally disreputable. [Laughter]
Sports touts are guys who tell you what team to bet on this weekend and
charge you for the privilege. They’re not hard to find. They’re in USA
Today. They’re on TV. They’re on radio. You just probably haven’t paid
any attention to them. This is a multi-billion industry. It’s somewhere
between nearly fraudulent and completely fraudulent. It’s huge.
What a lot of people don’t know is there are guys who played in lowest
common denominator. Their customer base is the blue collar guy who
works at a crappy job. You see a lot of those guys by the way advertising
— they sell picks by the game even at 900 numbers where it gets charged
to the guy’s phone number and for $25 they tell them whether Ohio State
is going to cover the spread Saturday or not.
The high level of that business is selling $10,000 to $100,000 packages for
the season. There’s a guy for example, now he’s completely disreputable
by the way. There are guys who are close to reputable. He is not one of
them. Every year shortly before the season begins. He begins to advertise
in the Wall Street Journal, Investor’s Business Daily, Barron’s, and Rob
Report, Worth and Forbes. Relatively small ads to generate leads. The
last time I talked to him he told me his lead cost was $3,000. To get
somebody to raise their hand from that environment to that type of ad cost
him $3,000.
He could care less. He closes one out of ten, which is a $30,000 cost per
sale. He makes a $50,000 sale. He has no product cost. All he’s doing is
delivering recorded messages every weekend. For the most part, unless
he’s incredibly lucky pretty much all of them have gone away by the
middle of the season. They’re not calling anymore because they lost.
Eventually you don’t even have to record the messages. [Laughter] He
makes a nice $2 million, $3 million every year. His customers are
doctors, lawyers, stock brokers, CEOs. He has a few times tried to bridge
the gap and “go legit” and move those same customers with him over to

manually yourself.

this particular place. In Florida, for example, risk converse people tend to
live in Central Florida. People less risk converse live on the coast.
Logical, less hurricanes in central Florida than on the coast.

available information. Legacy stuff, married more than single. Sure does
the divorced guy with five kids and 12 grandkids - yeah, but do you want

that really expensive tool catalogue, that says something about them.
Now that might match up with and in a psychological thing you believe is
true about your customer.
It’s never perfect, but it can be good enough. Hardly anybody does the
work to do it. The second thing is if you’re going to go direct to them,
you’ve got to get past a lot of nonsense that everybody believes. The first
thing that they believe the person you’re outreaching to, the affluent. The
first thing they believe is if you have to be chasing them than you can’t be
very good.
The first problem you have and almost everybody in here has this
problem. It’s just made worse the more affluent your prospect is. If he
prospects me as a financial advisor my little alarm bell starts going off.
Shit, if he has to be chasing clients how good could he be? Same thing
with you guys, same thing with Terry, same thing with just about
everybody.

When we do direct, so this is one of the problems is the minute we arrive
directly there is the sense of if they have to prospect they can’t be very
good. We know it’s there. We’ve got to confront it, put it on the table
and answer it. We can’t ignore it. You now have to decide what your
answer is to this problem and craft the best one you can. Put it on the
table fairly early in your communication with people. There’s all sorts of
answers by the way from mission statement and champion. I mean the list
is long and endless. But you’ve got to do it.
The next problem is that they don’t want anything that is for everybody.
The last thing they want. Anything that is - the more generic it is the less
likely it is to be read, paid attention to, and given any interest.
Female: Everybody you mean [inaudible]
Dan: Both. Price helps. Presentation helps. The first thing is yes they
don’t want it to be for everybody literally in the sense of everybody. They
don’t even want it to be for everybody in their peer group. The closest to
it’s for them the better you are. Price is a separator. It’s why often people
who are afraid of price out front or at least hint of price out front by the
very way something is presented.
I don’t have to — I don’t know that I guess $500,000 but I kind of know
this isn’t going to be cheap. I kind of know you’re not sending them out
by the truckload. You may be by the way but the natural thought that will
occur to me is you’re not sending these suckers out by the truckload.
A certain amount of both price desensitization and elitism, therefore it’s
for me and it’s not for everybody occurs the minute I take it out of the
package. That is no excuse for follow it up with bad copy. That’s not to
say there shouldn’t have been a 16 page sales letter with it. It’s not to say
there shouldn’t been a book of testimonials with it. There should be. The
guys selling the medical thing are better by the way. They have - I just

Dan: For the — you give them $500,000 and they manage your

we pay for our own because we won’t use the system we supported with
the taxes. Get ready to write the check. It’s only a question of time.
You have to find a way to clearly convey pretty quickly that you’re not
mass marketing. Now at the lead generation stage you can still be kind of
mass in some settings. Pretty quickly after that you better not be mass.
The less you look that way the better.
The next problem then is this trust issue. The more affluent the more we
know what they’re looking for. They’re not looking for a product.
They’re not looking for a service. They’re not looking for a simple
solution. Again, the guy that will respond to that show I described will
run in and buy. They’re pitching a product. This guy’s pitching an equity
indexed annuity.
A simple minded buyer if you will, that’s great. Somebody with a lot of
money has complex issues. I don’t want a product. I want a guy. Other
than the guy being real good on the show, he’s doing Andy Griffith folks.
He’s talking about hens and roosters. He’s funny. He’s not going to get
me because that’s not, I don’t want a product. I want somebody I can
trust. We know we’re in the trust business. Direct outreach is contrary to
trust. Trust is you tell me about Kurt or I ask you. Who do you know?
Who can, you know?
Male: How about lead generation? If you’re lead generating for a
magazine and they find you is that discovery or is that as you start bu…
Dan: It’s better than, yeah. Even lead generation direct mail. In the very
early stage you can get away with simply being compelling. As long as
it’s not jarringly wrong, people will respond. Then pretty quickly you’re
dealing with all these issues. The earlier you deal with them the better off
you’re going to be. We have to find ways to create trust unnaturally. The
natural way to get there is you tell me about a guy and I already trust you.
That’s the natural way to get there.
Let’s think about — so think about going to a computer dating site versus
being fixed up on a blind date by somebody you really know and trust.
How do they get somebody to go to a computer dating site? They try and
create as best they can a trust story.
If you listen to them everybody takes this 5,622 question thing, which just
their willingness to do that says that they are serious about finding a mate
not - they don’t say it exactly this way, but not just about getting in the
sack. They’re serious about finding a mate the very fact that they go

www.getwsodownload.com

-- 111 of 192 --

Marketing to the Affluent Transcript
Page 110
through this process. Then we have this proprietary whiz-bang Oz guy
behind the curtain that analyses all that crap and matches you etcetera,
etcetera. They do everything they can to tell a trust story where there’s no
trust.
Male: In a way does Google sort of ad to this too?
Dan: Yes, the very fact that you set up hurdles helps to raise trust, as
opposed to being real sales and trying to go right to the sale. Above
everything else we have to try and replicate pure recommendation. Social
proof in these settings is critically important.
If I’m going to get something from Kurt and I didn’t get him
recommended to me by anybody I know I responded one way or another
to something I got from him. The next best thing now is a lot of people
who are like somebody I would ask for a recommendation from, now
being delivered to me by Kurt. That’s like the next best thing.
If I’m a dentist, the only social proof that is persuasive are other dentists.
If he puts in front of me a bunch of mid size company CEOs, doesn’t do
anything. I probably don’t know any. That’s not who I would of asked. I
would have asked my buddy I went to dental school with who I still talk to
every week who’s smarter about money than I am. That’s who I would
have asked.
If you show me a bunch of people from small towns an I live on the 39th
floor of a high rise condo in Manhattan, no good. That’s not who I
would’ve asked. The real question here is who would I ask? Now you
have to try and replicate the who would I ask.
Another way around it is an authoritative figure. Everybody in financial
services evokes Warren Buffet. His name is probably spoken more now
in a day than Christ I imagine. [Laughter] Which incidentally, I think it’s
either you might know. It’s either 5.4 or 6.2 his losses over the last five
years, worst than the S&P 500. Just in case you think he’s a genius. Be
careful how you bet on it.
Authoritative figures that they would ask if they could, helps. If we were
going after somebody who would we go to lunch with if we could and ask
about money? Well the first thing that pops into just about everybody’s
head is Buffett. You have to - what you’re trying to do is unnaturally
create trust and at different levels.
The low level this is where celebrity works a lot. At lower levels people
have celebrity totally confused with credibility. You show them Hulk
Hogan in a vitamin, you’re good to go. You try and do that with really

affluent guys you’re in the tank.
If you look at what the really affluent — you’ve all seen the ad for

56 year old doctor, an MD who shows you his body which is the body of a
35 year old.

Enquirer selling to a lower common denominator customer. Conceptually

to show you the before because we all know the before. I are the before
[Laugher] Look at picture, look at me, look at picture, look at me. Gee
would you like to have that guy’s body? Any day of the freakin week.
Where do I sign? Those are lead generation ads aimed at affluent people.
One of the things that’s true about this like we talked about with the
machine. People have to see it more before they’ll respond. You can’t go
by me because I respond to see what they send me. That screws up the
whole process.
If you - in casual conversation I found five or six people I know who have
responded to the Synergenics ad. Is that how you say it?

It’s not a cheap sale.
Everybody who I know who has responded as voices that they have seen
the ad again and again and again starting from ignoring to resisting, to
reading and then not responding to tearing out and not responding, to
tearing out and finally responding. The curve to get them there is longer,
requires more exposure.

going on in their lives. There's a line of them people from here to the

caliber of guy. They've got teeth, they've got suits, they've got jobs.
They're still willing to come on Dr. Phil and tell the most amazing things.

workplace tomorrow, having just appeared on national television talking
about the most amazing things.

could get a CEO making a million a year to sit there and talk about how he
can't break his addiction to sleeping with goats, and it would still go on.

Dan: It wouldn't be as easy, it would be as - I actually have a joke, but I
won't do it. It wouldn't be as easy, but it's doable. One of the things that's
important to do is, when you are asking, is to talk about the use. So I am
less sensitive about it if it is not being broadcast everywhere, if I'm not

being disrespectful to anybody. [Laughter] Did anybody bother, probably
not, but saw center with you. Did anybody bother doing the homework
assignment I suggested to you of looking at the investment letter in your
manual and why I did not buy?
No, of course not, okay. It’s all right, that’s all right.
Male: You meant last night?
Dan: Yes, I meant last night. We’re not here tomorrow. That would sort
of telegraph too much credit huh?
Male: I read your politic stuff because that’s…
Dan: Too much credit, giving him too much credit. Yes, I meant last
night. That’s all right. However, you might want to do it on your own
because he almost got me. I don’t have time for you to read it while we’re
together. Instead, let’s get the one other biggy in that everybody wanted
to talk about as a general topic.
First of all, of course obviously everybody enjoyed and saw things in the
field trip that are interesting to you. What I did say in the car on the way
back is probably the most pronounced commonality of the three if you
think about it is their careful attention to language, all three of them. They
have deliberately chosen certain words over others. Ways of saying things
over others and do a pretty good job of sticking to it.
NOTES:
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 139 of 192 --

Marketing to the Affluent Transcript
Page 138
All right let’s talk about referrals. We know that, at least everybody tells
us, of course that the more affluence the individual has the more resistant
they are to cold prospecting, advertising, direct marketing. The more
likely they are to prefer having need or desire meant by someone referred
to them by a peer they already trust. That their first tendency is to go ask
somebody even when awakened by advertising, direct marketing or
whatever.
All of the research indicates that’s true. Anecdotally it appears to me to
be true. I’m willing to accept it as true. It is in a sense the best and the
worst feature of the affluent client. It is the best because they can be a far
better source of referrals of really good clients than any other type of
client because they are more likely to be asked by all of their peers about
just about everything. Given a good cocktail party story, they are most
likely to be telling to equivalently affluent individuals that can be
referrals.
The best thing about an affluent client is they have much greater value as a
multiplier than do less affluent. They have more influence with their
peers. Their peers are equally high value customers. This has to be taken
into consideration when you do your math and determine what you are
willing to spend to acquire a client. What you’re willing to spend on the
experience of the client. What you are willing to spend on retaining the
client.
It’s dangerous at the lower levels to play this game with math. To say
okay Billy Bob’s worth $1,000 a year as a client. I should be able to get
two referrals from Billy Bob. That makes Billy Bob really worth $3,000.
If each of those two refers three that really makes Billy Bob worth
whatever that number would be. I’m bad at math. It’s either $12,000 or
$22,000, whatever the number is.
Start to pencil yourself blind to the point that you’re spending way too
much money to get Billy Bob or way too much money taking care of Billy
Bob. Some customers and clients are only worth taken care of to a certain
extent.
It is ever more legitimate the further up this affluence ladder that we go to
take into consideration the value of them as multipliers. It’s their best
feature. It’s also their worst feature because it’s an impediment to those of
us who want to go out and get them directly. We talked about that earlier
today everything that has to happen to sort of simulate peer referral and
peer recommendation as best we can when we don’t have it.
As an aside, it’s the other reason why omnipresence is so important in a
market place because the narrower the niche or the narrower the sub-
NOTES:
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 140 of 192 --

Marketing to the Affluent Transcript
Page 139
culture. Then the more affluent the customers are within that niche or
sub-culture that we are selling to, the more likely they are when directly
approached and interested to now ask two or three people they know what
do you know about x, y, z. What do you know about Viking Appliances?
What do you know about Dan Kennedy? What do you know? Have you
ever heard of?
The worst response of course is a bad one. Oh yeah, I know a lot about
him. I wouldn’t wish it on my worst enemy. Let me tell you the 512
reasons why you ought not buy that thing. Almost as bad as the nope
never heard of him. It’s nearly as bad because it has the same effect.
Mostly it’s going to kill the person’s interest in moving forward towards
you based on the marketing that you did.
The immediate issue for me is if in my peer group nobody’s ever heard of
this product or nobody’s ever heard of this company or nobody’s ever
heard of this person, then they’re probably not dealing with my peer
group. They’re probably not for me.
The best possible response is of course, “Oh yeah I own one of those.
Yeah, I’ve done business with those guys and they’re great.” Certainly an
acceptable response is at least, “Yeah, I’ve heard of them. What I’ve
heard is blank.” Controlling that story that’s told about you, the short
story, the answer from people who don’t do business with you is really
really important.
It’s why omnipresence is so beneficial. If they’ve read articles. If they’ve
seen your book advertised. If they’ve seen that you’re speaking at this
thing even whether they went to it or not. If they’ve read an article about
you in a magazine. They begin to assign importance and relevance to you
even if they haven’t done business with you.
That worst answer is going to be, “Yeah, I’ve heard of him. Gees, he
seems to be everywhere.” Well that’s a whole lot better than no, I’ve
never heard of him. This is a best and a worst feature that has to be
managed. It is what it is. Understanding what it is tells us we have to go
out of our way to do certain things to try and control it.
Male: How much of that story should you be working to pass through?
Obviously you should have different versions of it there. There’s the full
legend and then there’s the what you’re trying — they’re not going to
retain all that obviously to pass along. Are we talking in your case
crotchety marketing guy who’s a genius and doesn’t take any shit back
that much?

any version of that.

Dan: Yeah. That’s mostly is kind of what’s out there. That’s fine. I had
a case, this is decades ago now. Greg Stanly who’s in chiro and dental
marketing. He’s the chiropractors, and markets to dentist. Basically
coach, consulting, marketing advisor.
Greg has been around a long time. Ten years ago if you asked, if you
stopped any 20 chiropractors mix their ages if you wanted to or any 20
dentist and said, “What do you know about Greg Stanly?” You would
almost always get — first of all you’d always get an answer. You would
always get a good reputation answer. That was the good news.
The bad news is the answer included the three things he did. The answer
was, “Oh, yeah never heard anything bad about him. My brother goes to
him. Stanly good guy. He’s the guy who tells you to pay off all your
credit cards, invest in municipal bonds and keep your practice overhead
below 30%.”
They all knew it so well that now why go to the seminar. You just gave
me the seminar. Furthermore, you gave me three pieces of good advice,
none of which I like. [Laughter.]
He had a pretty good personal brand. It had achieved a negative status
because all the mystery, all the curiosity, all the intrigue, all of that was
gone. We literally had to jack up the brand and create a whole new
message and a whole new business underneath it.
You have to be really sensitive to this. It’s why by the way marketing in

small circles is better than marketing in big circles because it’s easier to
control it. It’s easier to be everywhere when everywhere is Macedonia,

entire restaurant industry. It’s easier to be everywhere and to try and
exercise control over this thing.

Dan: There’s has to be a core thing that you decide to stay with. You
want sort of the most casual observer who might be in conversation about
you to have an understanding of.
It was interesting to hear her mention her KISS experience yesterday at

Pre-paying attention to Gene Simmons because of A & E. I’m not a rock

Dan: No, the benefits implied. Yeah, no I don’t think it has have a

can exercise an enormous amount of control over.
Second thing, the biggest point of leverage in multiplying these types of
clients by referrals, I knew I had it here somewhere, is the cocktail party
story is the biggest point of leverage we have. They use them a lot. The
ones they like they tend to polish and perfect and use over and over and
over again.
It’s helpful to be providing one of those. You’re not local so it won’t do
him much good. Probably of the three places that we went, although
everybody’s doing a lot of right things. Everybody did a very nice job for
us. Probably the one that would get turned into the story from you is the
Pet Place. That would be my wager.
He won that war. You’re only going to tell so many cocktail party stories
this month. You’re only going to keep one or two that have long life.
How do we create that? That’s what we want. Somebody asked me about
Simmons last night at dinner. Was he worth the money to have at the
Super Conference? He certainly worth the money because of this. You
can’t evaluate it. Did you get his feedback in registrations you wouldn’t
have had otherwise. A, no I don’t think so.
I think there was some that were nudged over purely because of it. I don’t

buying mood. I don’t know how you quantify that because you don’t have
any kind of decent split test. You have four hundred other variables year
to year. How do you know?
You anecdotally you know. Somebody comes up and says I haven’t been
to super conference in five years. I wasn’t coming. I came because of
Simmons. They have two big bags full of crap. Well you can go okay,
there’s $1800. I mean it’s pretty unscientific.
The one thing we do know is cocktail party story value after the event -
huge, huge. Everybody’s talking about it to everybody they knew.
Everybody running home, putting a picture up on the website. Emailing
their list, “Come see the picture of me with Gene Simmons.” Worth twice
the fee.

This is a big thing for those of you in the event business. This is a big

as the go to guy they can get somebody else to. That’s extremely
powerful. It gives him status to have in his pocket the guy he can get you
to that you can’t get to on your own. That’s really powerful. There’s
these kinds of caches. I had — for many years I did not make much. I did
not make money comparable to money I got from any other client. My
fees rapidly out grew where we started.
Usage went down with over time with Guthy-Renker. Guthy-Renker was
extremely useful to me because they were Guthy-Renker. The association
was valuable and the fact that I could get anybody to Greg. If I overused
it I would have worn out the welcome. There was and there still is a
certain level of tolerance there for…
Tom Shack comes to me thinking he wants to do a weight loss
infomercial. I can get him on the phone with Renker. Whether anything’s
going to happen or not is really not the issue. I can get him on the phone
to Renker. He hasn’t got prayer one on getting on the phone probably
Renker on his own and would quickly discover that if he tried it.
This is something your referrer wants, is he wants to be a conduit. He
wants to have access that he can play, has a card from time to time that
makes him look good.

There’s a profound difference between the two.
What you did was A+ as a testimonial. It has all the elements most people
leave out of their testimonials. But it was not a cocktail party story.

Dan: Yeah, years ago, now we changed it. This is kind of interesting.
Ronny Townsend and I were selling a $30,000 coaching program. We
didn’t call it coaching then. We called it practice management. A
$30,000 program to chiropractors in 1979. At the time it was the highest
priced thing in the industry. We brought them into what we know call
Discovery Days. We didn’t call it that then. We brought them in for two
days in Florida and pitched them.
One of the things that we did at the hotel. We only did it this way once
and then we learned something. I’ll tell you what we learned. We had the
local exotic car dealer bring over a couple of Rolls, couple of Maseratis,
ten cars. Put them in a big circle out in the parking lot. At about 4:00
p.m. on day one we took everybody out and made them go sit in the cars,
smell the cars, pick out the one they wanted, feel what it was like to sit
behind a wheel. all that justified as mind expanding, bigger thinking
exercises.
Really, amongst other things I wanted them going home telling a story.

Marketing to the Affluent Transcript
Page 145
They never did that before at a seminar. They’re going to go home and
some doctor buddy of theirs is going to say what was the seminar like?
Now they have story to tell. Well, gees, you’re not going to believe this. I
sat in a, they brought all of these cars over and I mean I was in a Maserati
Goldwing. The thing’s $246,000. My God, I’m going to sell my wife and
my three kids because I want one of them Maseratis. He’s got a story to
go home and tell.
We knew it was going to be a great story. We also thought it would help
get their thinking up. When they get hit with a $30,000 price tag
tomorrow, it now has different frame of reference.
The first — we did that by the way. Then we took them the guy who
owned the company who was a fruit loop by the way. He had a big yacht
on the inner coastal waterway. His house was right on. We put them all
on the yacht and took them over to his house. He played the grand piano.
His wife came down the steps in a big gown. It was a big production. To
say some of it was just to appease him. Still it put price in it. It put price
in a different frame of reference for the next day.
We learned something though. We learned the rolls Royce and the
Mazeratis make for great cocktail party story but it was too much. They
couldn’t really see themselves in them. Every seminar after that we had
one Rolls. We had a few Cadillacs. We had a few Lincolns. We had
Lexus, didn’t even exist I don’t think in ‘79 did it. We had top luxury cars
but cars they could think of themselves as being in. The only Rolls we
brought over was actually Doc S’s. We brought his car over. We didn’t
get that dealer. We got the Lincoln dealer. We got the Cadillac dealer.
We had something else. I don’t remember what the third thing was.
It still worked as a great story. It worked better for the other thing. See
that’s different than having ten testimonials stand up and tell their success
story. There’s a place for that too. This is really — what are they going
to talk? What are they going to tell their wife if she’s not there? What are
they going to sell her on the phone tonight?
Well a seminar is a seminar. If I give you cookies or I don’t give you
cookies. If you got good content, if you didn’t get. If the workbook was
thick or thin. A couple of days from now nobody remembers any of that.
It’s not anything you’re going to run home and tell somebody.
If you’re going to tell somebody, what are you going to tell them about?
Well you’re going to tell them about well the limousine had a fireplace in
it. The visit to the dog place. That’s what you’re going to tell somebody
about. You’re not going to tell somebody about this. The seminar
comment is going to be, “Yeah, it was a great seminar. Oh, yeah I got a

lot of notes. Oh, yeah I got a lot of ideas.” Everybody’s heard that.
They’ve heard that before.
Male: How important is that in a cocktail party story concept after you’ve

someone coaching high price coaching. They come to that, part of that as
a mastermind meeting or a get together something. They come to that…
Dan: It is completely unimportant in satisfying them. The more they
spend, the less important it is. Same thing with a seminar. Somebody
pays $10,000 to come to a seminar and you don’t have to do anything. I
don’t have to feed you. It doesn’t matter what hotel we’re in. You came
to work. You came for the right reasons.
If I charged you $500 to come a seminar you’re going to be pissing and
moaning there’s not five flavors of juice. Where’s my notebook. You
have to give them all that crap. Ironically cheaper.
My platinum coaching guys get less than my VIP guys ever did. I don’t
— screw that. That’s not what we’re here for. They don’t care. They
would sit on the floor and you wouldn’t hear anything from them. If you
want them referring, if you want referrals, then this stuff is all real
important. Well let me get to it. There’s reasons why people don’t refer.
Our first big point of leverage is the cocktail party story. I have this
projector really screwed up, don’t I? It’s like I’m drunk.
The second biggest story. I took a bunch of Nyquil. I am drunk. Second
biggest story is I can get you into this guy. I have this guy in my pocket.
As soon as he has to be gotten into this guy because of you he’s his
readiness to purchase goes way up. The referrer just got to look important
because I can get you into this guy. You can’t, you probably can’t get into
him on your own but I can get you in. Let me make a call for you.
Have you ever read Michael Corta’s book called Power? It’s an old book.
If you haven’t, you should get it if you can find it. Corta talked about one
of the most powerful phrases in the world is, “Let me make a call for
you.” In front of someone you are on the phone making this access
happen or making this introduction happen. “Let me make a call for you.”
It’s very very powerful. The person’s indebted to you forever. You’ve
got to show. You had status because you had the guy you could call.
The guy who can be called who’s positioned that way is likely to get more
referrals. I get something out of it when I refer. What do I get when I
refer? I get status of being the guy who can connect you with this guy. I
get a lot of client referrals for this reason. That’s why I get them. I’ve
NOTES:
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 148 of 192 --

Marketing to the Affluent Transcript
Page 147
made myself inaccessible by most means. In fact, people try and end run
my process this way.
People get power because of it. They’re always going to Milteer. It
doesn’t work by the way, for the most part. Every once in awhile
somebody will get her to do something. A lot of them try it. There’s a lot
of them who want to start doing business with her because they think
they’re going to be able to get access to me through her.
She can play the card anytime she wants to. I have a certain level of
tolerance even for talking to people that I know are pointless. I know she
won’t be playing the card every day. Whenever she does play it, she’s
gained status and power with that person’s that she’s dealing with. She
likes being able to play it. I get referrals because people like to play that
card. They know they can’t just play it indiscriminately or I’ll take it
away from them. I get good referrals more often than not.
Why they don’t refer? I’m coming back to where we were. The first
reason that people don’t refer in general and the first biggest reason that
affluent people don’t refer is they don’t get asked. Affluent people get
asked less because their clients are afraid of them, intimidated by them.
They don’t want to ask them to refer. Mostly they do not get asked.
Often just being asked properly is enough. You have to be careful not to
ask in a way that makes you look weak. You can’t afford to look weak
with an affluent client. Even at the high end, and even dumb stuff will
work to a certain extent.
The guy that has my pension accounts, Dan Wiggins, is mostly dealing
with guys with investable assets of $5 million or up. He routinely in his
newsletter asks for referrals. Every once in a while runs a promotion. A
few months ago he ran some kind of thing and gave away some whiz-bang
coin anybody that referred. Now that doesn’t — I refer people to him
periodically because he does a good job. So far he hasn’t stolen all my
money or lost most of it. I consider that a major accomplishment.
I don’t care about the coin. Somebody else did it because that was enough
of a nudge. Most guys in his position just don’t ask anybody at all. They
don’t do it. That’s the first reason that affluent people don’t refer is they
don’t get asked.
The second, back to where we were. This is again, this applies to
everybody. It’s sort of on steroids with the affluent client. They don’t
because they’re satisfied but not enthused, wowed, blown away.
Whatever terminology you want to use. We all have businesses we deal
with with whom we are sufficiently satisfied to continue doing business
NOTES:
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 149 of 192 --

Marketing to the Affluent Transcript
Page 148
with them.
On a real germane level, think about your dry cleaners. There’s like a
basic commodity level. Pretty much people stay with their dry cleaners
unless or until the dry cleaners screw something up so bad. Usually they
have to do it more than once that they are just driven to a point of
screaming rage. Then they go find another dry cleaner.
Are any of you patronizing? Well first of all how many of you have been
using the same dry cleaner for six month or longer? A year or longer?
Who in here has referred anybody to their dry cleaner in the last three
months? Six months? A year? Here we are.
Well, there’s your demonstration. Everybody’s satisfied, nobody’s
referring. Why? Satisfaction does not prompt referrals, that’s why. If it
did everybody would have raised their hands twice. Then the dry cleaner
would be getting a lot of referrals.
If what’s supposed to happen happens, that’s not enough. It’s enough to
keep them. It’s enough to satisfy them. Again, I don’t care about it. I
don’t worry about it. Guy’s coming to a platinum meeting see it doesn’t
matter how good that meeting is as a meeting. How much they walk out
of there with. How much money they make as a result of it. That is not
going to get me a referral. If I wanted referrals I’ve got to back up this
list. I have to worry about a lot of all this other stuff.
I’m the dry cleaner. They’re perfectly satisfied. They’ll renew until the
end of time. I’ll never hear any complaining from them. They’re all
getting great value. I’m not doing anything that would cause them to feel
motivated, compelled, obligated. I’m not giving them a story to go home
from the meeting that they have got to get on the phone and call ten of
their buddies, who would five of them would then be calling my office
saying how the hell do I get into that platinum thing? I’m not doing that.
Male: It makes it almost a joke when you hear these normal small
medium size businesses who say they only refer rely on referrals to get
business even more so. You know they’re not doing. They’re barely
satisfying if that.
Dan: No, that’s right. That’s right. They better be delivering. They
actually, they don’t even mean referrals when they say it. They mean
word of mouth advertising which really is they’re two actually, they’re
two different things. Even word of mouth advertising generally is not
sparked …
Male: By that, satisfaction.

Dan: Yeah. At best for you to refer that dry cleaner somebody would
have to specifically ask you. Somebody new moves in next door to you
comes over and says, “Where do you get your cleaning done?” You might
very well say, “Well I go over here.” He counts that as a referral. Think
about what had to happen for it to occur. It’s not like you’re blown away.
By the way it would be very easy for a dry cleaner to do it. They just,
they just don’t. Nobody does.
If I wanted them all going home and jumping on the phone and calling
their buddies I would have to do things differently than I do. Fundamental
satisfaction is not enough to breed referrals. Cocktails parties breed
referrals. Creating a status access thing breeds referrals. Wow
experiences breed referrals. Good food breeds referrals.
I always joke, if you’re going to do anything at the meeting make sure you
have really good cookies. Cookies matter more than anything else.
Male: Is that we got No BS cookies?
Dan: That’s why, that limo company did that on their own. I had nothing

Male: We see it differently. You guys are competing.
Dan: Same schools, same school district, etcetera. The key word here is
occasionally. Most people actually are not that way. If they are motivated
they’re going to tell their peers about this person, thing, place, they’ve
discovered, almost without regard to it perhaps being in some way shape
or form competitive with their interests. It isn’t anything you can fix.
You ignore it. It’s occasional anyway.
The last main reason they don’t refer is no recognition, no reward. Let’s
take recognition first. It’s astounding, but for the most part people don’t
get thanked pretty much for anything. It’s amazing people do refer and
then don’t get thanked. Generally speaking, everybody thinks they are
unappreciated and not getting the recognition that they ought to get.

ways to deal with this.

We think, let’s take a guy like Trump. We think a guy like Trump’s
walking around with everybody kissing his butt all the time. Surely the
guy feels honored, and respected, and appreciated. I guarantee you half
the time he’s like stomping around the office. “Nobody appreciates
anything I do around here. Why in the hell haven’t I gotten a thank you
note in the last four days from these 86 people that I did something for?”
Just because they’re loaded doesn’t mean they’re distanced from these
fundamental human emotions. One is we all think we should have been
thanked by now for something we did for somebody yesterday and the
week before, and the week before that.
Every one of you if we talked about it now which we won’t because of

guy worth $50 million. He’s just a little more grumpy about it than we
are. He really thinks he should have been thanked.
The smartest thing you could ever do when you get a referral or for that
matter some other kind of assistance, cooperation from someone who has
no particular reason to give it to you. The smartest thing that you could do
is be effusive in recognition and appreciation because they will be
incredibly motivated to do it again.
They like that feeling. They will now do it again and we already know
they know how to do it because they did it once. We don’t have to train
them. Much easier to get second referral from somebody who’s already
given you one than it is to get the first one from somebody’s who’s never
given you any. They don’t know how. This guy already showed me he
knows how to do it. I know he knows more than one.

means your newsletter. That means some hand written notes, something
torn out of a magazine. Gees, Rob, I thought you should see this. That

The thing you have to understand and it kind of links back to the

inner circle tends to be smaller but significant. Referring is high stakes for
them. If I refer you to somebody and it goes badly I may or may not hear
about it. I’ll probably hear about it until we drop dead if you’re like a
friend, friend.

colors the relationship from here on out. That affluent person is reluctant
to refer because so much is at stake. If I told my neighbor about my dry
cleaners and the dry cleaners fucks up their cleaning. I may hear about it
but so what.
If I tell a client that they should go to their Vision Day, I’m on the hook.
I’ve put an important relationship at stake here for no particular gain.
When I do do that not only do I want a report back. I want to hear that
things went well. I expect some love. I expect some appreciation. I’m
disappointed when I don’t get it. I think most of us are.

Dan: Of course it made sense. Of course it made sense. Last thing about
this. Everybody has a moral scale. 5% of the people don’t. 5% of the
people are hardwired to be dishonest no matter what. They have no
internal system like this at all. But 95% of people do.
They have a certain tipping point in every relationship where a sense of
obligation occurs and begins to feel uncomfortable if it is not discharged.
It’s different for every person. It’s different in every relationship.
Let’s think about going out to lunch. You and I go out to lunch and you
pick up the tab. Now three weeks later we go out to lunch and either you
get to it first or I left my wallet at the office. Now you’ve picked up the
tab twice. Three, four weeks we go out to dinner, you, Carla, I, you pick
up. At some point, and you should hope sooner not later in this particular
scenario, at some point the little thing goes off in my head that says this is
way out of kilter. I’ve got to do something about this. I don’t want this
debt building up in a way that is uncomfortable to either one of us.
That would occur for all of us. For some it might be the second, for others
it might be the fifth time. At some point it’s going to occur. It’s why it’s
really hard. You all have this too. It’s really hard to have a long time
friend or a close family member who is nowhere near as financially
successful as you are. It makes it very difficult to do a lot of things with
them. They can’t carry their load or even come close to it.
Not only does it make them uncomfortable, it makes you uncomfortable.
You don’t mind it. You’re happy to do it. That still, it isn’t right.
NOTES:
www.getwsodownload.com

Marketing to the Affluent Transcript
Page 158
Everybody has this going on about every relationship. Guy next door has
a snow plow, you don’t. He comes over and plows your drive when
there’s a great big snow storm. Well once it’s like, okay he’s a neighbor
great thanks, Bob. I appreciate it.
At some point, second snow storm, third snow storm, you depending upon
- you might offer him money which he probably turns down. At some
point you’re going to buy him a case of beer. Your wife she’s going to
bake him something. You’re going to do something to try and even that
up. It’s automatic. It’s in here for every relationship.
In relationships where it is to your advantage you want to keep getting it
to the point that the other guy has to discharge obligation as quickly and as
often as you can. For the most part, how can a client customer, patient
discharge obligation.
Hair stylist I can tip her. I can over tip. My chiropractor I can’t. You
don’t tip your chiropractor. You don’t tip your dentist. You don’t tip
your attorney. How else do I discharge this obligation?
Sometimes you see it done. A guy calls you up and says, “I have four
tickets on the floor and our company can’t use them. You want to go to
the game.” What’s he really doing? He’s discharging some obligation
that’s been built up.
Male: What if you don’t take them?
Dan: That’s the guy we’ll just take the tickets. [Laughter] The easiest
way for a client or customer to discharge that obligation is what? Refer.
Of course the easiest thing they can do.
Male: Cross them off.
Dan: A lot of referral activity is driven by obligation hitting the tipping
point. Instead of it happening accidentally you can think about it. You
can make this happen. Even sending them stuff they should see which
applies to many of our businesses. I institutionalized that in platinum. I
don’t institutionalize it with others. I’ve always done that. I do it a lot.
It’s cheap. It’s easy. I read a lot of stuff anyway.
I’ve always noticed, for years I would notice with Renker it was almost to

page 57. His number’s like nine. Nine and I either get a call with some
kind of project to do or I get a referral. There’s somebody Greg Renker
sent me and will you do this and will you do that?

name we wouldn’t do it again. We’ve also on the offer certificate we give
the winner 30 days to contact us just in case we don’t get the name. That
has created a much greater and quicker response from the winners.
We have a lot of auction certificates out there now that have been out for
almost a year. They still haven’t claimed them. Now that we’ve started
the 30 day to keep it active they’re calling. In fact we’ve got one call on
the 30th day from the daughter of the winner because the daughter or the
winner was out of town. He wanted to make sure he didn’t lose it.

they’d like us all. There’s also some clients who couldn’t who would find
you not a tick stuffy but tight assed and Boston conservative. Wouldn’t
like you at all whom might like me or might like him and vice versa.
There’s some clients who would run from me who would find you right
up their alley. If anything I would lighten it up a little. Overall, it seems
to me that it is compatible with everything else that you do. Interesting
read, covers… I didn’t say anything major that I would monkey with.

Dan: It’s the two of them.
Male: We flip back and forth the main article.
Male: What we do is obviously I have a different personality style than
this guy does. Mine is more [inaudible] what we do is we write one half
the newsletter is right side up. One half of the newsletter is upside down.
It’s half written by him and half written by me. We try to keep an
editorial schedule that we’re sort of writing roughly on a same topic.
Its same topic two perspectives, two different personality style.
Female: We thought about doing that too. He said she said but…

Dan: It’s very important to highlight the clients. The newsletter is a
vehicle for recognition, demonstration of results, encouragement of
ascension all of that. That really is its primary value. A lot of people have
too much content and not enough customer in it. They’re happier with
less content more customers.

Male: We’ve been working on trying to change the language in our

Dan: The only difference would be and if there are different people
visiting the website than are visiting, than are being visited by the other
media.
Male: If you sent them directly it would be the same?
Dan: Same. Yeah, absolutely the same. Again people get — they mess
this up a lot. They think that different media has different rules and
predominantly it doesn’t. I will — application is it is easier to get
somebody to sit and read eight pages in a form like this than it is to get
them to read eight pages on the net.
I’ve got no buttons. The problem with the net is it’s like television.
Essentially I’ve got a clicker or a mouse or whatever. The temptation is
enormous. They just did this study of workers. The average office
worker with a computer on their desk interrupts what they are doing
voluntarily of their own volition every three and a half minutes all day
long.
The longest span of time they stay on anything is three and a half minutes.
Not because anybody is causing it, they’re doing it to themselves. They’re
going from what they were doing to YouTube to look at a funny video.
Then they’re going over here and buying something at Amazon. Then
they’re sending somebody an email. Then they’re watering the plant.
Then they’re scratching their butt. Then they’re looking at a catalogue
and then they’re - every three and a half minutes.
If you take the computer away it’s every 11 minutes. For I think obvious
reasons. A long form sales letter on the web which used to work, used to
just be able to stick the thing up there. For the most part, unless it is a
highly motivated reader rabidly interested in this topic that doesn’t work
anymore.
You can still deliver the same content. You have to put video with it.
You have to put audio with it. You have to put them in clumps. You have
to work harder to get them to read it. The verbiage itself, same person,
same language.
Just as it would be — see if you take a sales letter and you have to do

them right into continuity with no fees.
Tucker’s old model. I’m not charging any front fee. I’m putting them
into continuity at $500 a month or $600 a month or whatever the hell I’m
doing. You can spring that on them. They didn’t have to have a
conversation with anybody about the financial commitment. The financial
commitment is fundamentally non existent. Everybody’s credit card will
take it or they wouldn’t have been in the room in the first place. It’s no
more stressful a buying experience than going in the back of the room and
buying a box of stuff.
If you’re going to pop them for $20,000 or $30,000 or $40,000 and you’re
going to spring it on them there’s very few audiences that that’s going to
work with to any significant degree. $20,000, $30,000, $40,000
somebody’s got to think about. Somebody’s got to probably have
discussed it with her before he spends $30,000, $40,000, at least the
second time. [Laughter]

there are certain transactions that should be discussed before they are

seminar illusion to it.
There was so much financing available. Really the $30,000 was a flip
from a percentage above base where they were agreeing to give us 30% of
everything their practice increased by over three years. That was really
what was being sold.
Now the sitting down across from you at the desk was how much is your
practice doing now? X. We increase you buy 20% a year and there’s
12% inflation. At the end of the three years you will have paid us for this
program $412,630. If you prepay it you can make it go away for $36,000
and we have financing. Really it’s no money down and it’s $312 a month.
Well we could spring that on them. If we haven’t been springing $30,000
and you have to write a check. You have to put this on a credit card in
three pay. We had a lot of trouble. Other than that there’s no difference
in the two approaches. Ultimately, you’ll wind up in the same place with
one over the other.
The full disclosure approach is slower. The enormous benefit of it and
don’t take this in anyway other than with love and affection. One of the
benefits of the full disclosure approach is you don’t have to be that great
running the meeting. They already knew before they got there what the
program was. They knew what the price was. They knew what the deal
was. You don’t have to be Joe super closer running that meeting. You
have to screw it up. Pretty much 90% of them came intending to do it.
The other way you have to be pretty good. If you’re really, really good.

necessarily be in their income level and don’t mention it?
Dan: Yeah, sure you it’s better than nothing.
Female: Travel to us. Not a local person but somebody whose travels to
us. In secondary can we use people that they have already trust
themselves? Let’s say for example a connection that we have. It’s a
coach that runs a camp and use that person to refer us. Meaning if they
trust them to send their kids to Vanderbilt can we use the Vanderbilt
coach?
Dan: Sure.
Female: In that way?
Dan: As long as he’s not doing anything that’s in your way. Meaning
doing stuff really cheap. As long as he’s, not when he finds out you’re
selling a $30,000 deal that’s he’s not going to think you’re satanic and
now be sabotaging you if somebody turns around. Given that he’s.
Female: Cutting him in on the deal.
Dan: You can technically, legally it requires disclosure. I can’t imagine
you getting in trouble with what you’re doing.
Female: We could?
Dan: Yes.
Female: Those would be two ways we could get to some more people.
Dan: Last quick question and then we’re late. We need to pack up.
